▸ 10.1 10.1 43
▸ 10.1.01 Abcde Approach And Initial Assessment

Related: [[Shock - Overview]], [[Airway Management]], [[Acute Respiratory Failure]]

[!important]
The ABCDE approach is the universal systematic method for assessing and treating any acutely ill or deteriorating patient. It must be repeated until the patient is stable.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Perform a rapid, structured ABCDE assessment
  • Identify life-threatening problems in order of priority
  • Initiate immediate life-saving interventions
  • Know when to call for help / escalate

2. The ABCDE Sequence

A – Airway

  • Assess patency (talking, stridor, secretions, GCS)
  • Interventions: head tilt-chin lift, jaw thrust, suction, airway adjuncts, intubation if needed

B – Breathing

  • Look, listen, feel (RR, SpO₂, effort, chest movement, breath sounds)
  • Interventions: high-flow O₂, bag-mask ventilation, chest drain for tension PTX

C – Circulation

  • Pulse, BP, capillary refill, skin colour, JVP, heart sounds, ECG
  • Interventions: IV access, fluid challenge, haemorrhage control, CPR if pulseless

D – Disability

  • AVPU or GCS, pupils, blood glucose, seizures
  • Interventions: glucose, benzodiazepines for seizures

E – Exposure

  • Full exposure while preventing hypothermia
  • Look for rash, wounds, drains, abdomen, legs, back

3. Key Principles

  • Treat problems as you find them (do not complete full assessment before acting)
  • Reassess after every intervention
  • Call for help early (MET / ICU / senior)
  • Use monitoring (pulse oximeter, ECG, BP) from the start

4. Red Flags Requiring Immediate Escalation

  • Airway compromise
  • SpO₂ <90% despite O₂
  • SBP <90 mmHg
  • GCS <12 or falling
  • Lactate >4 mmol/L

5. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • The ABCDE approach is not a one-time assessment — it is repeated
  • Always assume cervical spine injury in trauma until proven otherwise
  • Hypoglycaemia is a reversible cause of altered consciousness — check glucose early

6. Common Viva Questions

  1. Outline the ABCDE approach.
  2. What are the signs of airway obstruction?
  3. When would you perform a needle thoracocentesis?
  4. What reversible causes of cardiac arrest are assessed in ?

7. One-Page Revision Summary

Airway → Breathing → Circulation → Disability → Exposure

Treat as you find problems. Reassess. Escalate early.

8. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • ABCDE sequence and interventions for each step
  • Treat problems as you find them (don't complete then treat)
  • Reassess after every intervention
  • Call for help early (MET/ICU)
  • Hypoglycaemia = reversible cause of altered consciousness

Should Know

  • Cervical spine protection in trauma
  • Red flags for escalation (airway compromise, SpO₂<90%, SBP<90, GCS<12, lactate>4)
  • Monitoring from start (SpO₂, ECG, BP)

Nice to Know

  • Paediatric ABCDE differences
  • Obstetric ABCDE modifications
  • Pre-hospital vs in-hospital approach variations

9. MCQs (10)

  1. In the ABCDE approach, which comes FIRST?

A. Breathing

B. Circulation

C. Airway

D. Disability

  1. A patient is talking normally. Airway assessment:

A. Intubate immediately

B. Airway is patent

C. Insert oropharyngeal airway

D. Perform tracheostomy

  1. During Breathing assessment, which is NOT checked?

A. Respiratory rate

B. SpO₂

C. Blood glucose

D. Chest movement

  1. Tension pneumothorax — immediate intervention during :

A. Chest X-ray

B. Needle thoracocentesis (2nd ICS mid-clavicular)

C. Intubation

D. High-flow oxygen only

  1. Circulation assessment includes all EXCEPT:

A. Pulse, BP, capillary refill

B. Skin colour, JVP

C. Blood glucose

D. ECG

  1. Disability — which is assessed?

A. AVPU or GCS, pupils, blood glucose

B. Airway patency

C. Breath sounds

D. Urine output

  1. Exposure — key principle:

A. Keep patient covered at all times

B. Full exposure while preventing hypothermia

C. Expose only the injured area

D. No exposure needed if CT planned

  1. ABCDE should be repeated:

A. Once only on admission

B. After every intervention

C. Only if patient deteriorates

D. Every 24 hours

  1. Hypoglycaemia as reversible cause of altered consciousness — when to check glucose?

A. Only if diabetic

B. Early in Disability for ALL altered consciousness

C. After CT head

D. Never in acute setting

  1. Red flag for immediate escalation in ABCDE:

A. SpO₂ 94% on oxygen

B. SBP 110 mmHg

C. GCS 14

D. Lactate >4 mmol/L

10. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 65-year-old man found confused at home. GCS 12 (E3V4M5), RR 28, SpO₂ 90% on air, BP 90/60, HR 110, Temp 38.5°C. Immediate priority:

A. CT head

B. ABCDE — Airway support, high-flow O₂, IV access, fluids, glucose

C. Lumbar puncture

D. Antibiotics only

  1. A trauma patient with stridor, unable to speak, saturating 85%. First action:

A. Intubate immediately (senior help)

B. Nebulised adrenaline

C. Chest X-ray

D. Sit up and observe

  1. During assessment, absent breath sounds left side, tracheal deviation right, hypotensive. Diagnosis and action:

A. Pneumonia — antibiotics

B. Tension pneumothorax — immediate needle decompression

C. Pleural effusion — chest drain

D. PE — anticoagulation

  1. A septic patient, lactate 5.2, BP 80/50 after 1L fluid. ABCDE action:

A. More fluids only

B. Start noradrenaline (vasopressor)

C. Wait for central line

D. Transfuse blood

  1. An unconscious diabetic patient, GCS 8. Disability — first check:

A. Pupils only

B. Blood glucose (reversible!)

C. CT head

D. Start insulin

  1. ABCDE principle — "treat as you find" means:

A. Complete full ABCDE, then treat

B. Treat life-threatening problems immediately when found at each step

C. Treat only after consultant review

D. Treat only in ICU

  1. A patient with anaphylaxis — Airway swelling, wheeze, hypotension. Adrenaline route:

A. IV 1mg

B. IM 0.5mg anterolateral thigh

C. SC 0.5mg

D. Nebulised

  1. Cervical spine protection in trauma ABCDE:

A. Only if neck pain

B. Assume injury until proven otherwise — immobilise during

C. Only if neurological deficit

D. Not part of ABCDE

  1. Reassessment after intervention in ABCDE:

A. Not needed if patient stable

B. Mandatory after EVERY intervention

C. Only at end of ABCDE

D. Only if requested by senior

  1. Monitoring during ABCDE:

A. Apply after ABCDE complete

B. From start: SpO₂, ECG, BP, RR

C. Only in ICU

D. Optional

11. Flashcards

  • Q: ABCDE order

A: Airway → Breathing → Circulation → Disability → Exposure

  • Q: Treat as you find

A: Intervene immediately at each step for life-threatening problems — don't complete then treat

  • Q: Airway patent check

A: Talking normally = patent

  • Q: Tension pneumothorax in

A: Needle decompression 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line (clinical diagnosis)

  • Q: Hypoglycaemia in

A: Check glucose EARLY in all altered consciousness — reversible

  • Q: Reassess

A: After EVERY intervention

  • Q: Cervical spine in trauma

A: Assume injury until proven otherwise; immobilise during Airway

  • Q: Red flags for escalation

A: Airway compromise, SpO₂<90%, SBP<90, GCS<12, lactate>4

12. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: C — Airway always first.

MCQ 2: B — Talking = patent airway.

MCQ 3: C — Blood glucose is , not .

MCQ 4: B — Tension PTX = clinical diagnosis, immediate needle decompression.

MCQ 5: C — Blood glucose is .

MCQ 6: A — = AVPU/GCS, pupils, glucose.

MCQ 7: B — Full exposure + prevent hypothermia.

MCQ 8: B — Reassess after every intervention.

MCQ 9: B — Check glucose early in ALL altered consciousness.

MCQ 10: D — Lactate >4 = red flag.

SBA 1: B — ABCDE first: Airway (GCS 12 may need support), Breathing (O₂), Circulation (fluids/vasopressors), Disability (glucose), Exposure.

SBA 2: A — Stridor + unable to speak + hypoxia = airway obstruction → intubate.

SBA 3: B — Tension PTX: absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation + hypotension = immediate needle decompression.

SBA 4: B — Lactate rising after fluid = fluid unresponsive → start noradrenaline.

SBA 5: B — Glucose first in — reversible cause.

SBA 6: B — Treat as you find problems immediately.

SBA 7: B — Anaphylaxis = IM adrenaline 0.5mg anterolateral thigh first-line.

SBA 8: B — Assume C-spine injury in all trauma until cleared.

SBA 9: B — Reassess after every intervention.

SBA 10: B — Monitoring (SpO₂, ECG, BP) from start.

▸ 10.1.02 Acid Base And Electrolyte Emergencies

Related: [[Acute Kidney Injury in Critical Illness]], [[Diabetic Emergencies (DKA and HHS)]], [[Critical Care Monitoring]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Fluid Resuscitation Strategies]]

[!tip]
Henderson-Hasselbalch (traditional) vs Stewart/Fencl (strong ion difference) approach. Systematic ABG interpretation: pH → PaCO₂ → HCO₃ → compensation → anion gap. Electrolyte emergencies: K⁺ (hyper/hypo), Na⁺ (hyper/hyponatraemia), Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, PO₄. Time-critical: hyperK >6.5, hyponatraemia <120 with seizures, Ca²⁺ >3.5 or <1.8. Key FCPS/MRCP: ABG stepwise interpretation, anion gap + delta-delta, Winter's formula, hyperK emergency treatment, hyponatraemia correction limits, Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ emergencies.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Interpret ABG systematically (traditional + Stewart approach)
  • Identify primary disorders and appropriate compensation
  • Calculate anion gap, delta-delta, strong ion difference
  • Manage hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia, hyper/hypocalcaemia, hyper/hypomagnesaemia, phosphate emergencies
  • Apply correction rate limits to avoid osmotic demyelination / cardiac arrhythmias

2. ABG Interpretation — Systematic Approach

Stepwise Algorithm

  1. pH → acidaemia (<7.35) or alkalaemia (>7.45)
  2. PaCO₂ → respiratory component (↑ = respiratory acidosis, ↓ = respiratory alkalosis)
  3. HCO₃⁻ → metabolic component (↓ = metabolic acidosis, ↑ = metabolic alkalosis)
  4. Primary vs Compensatory → match direction of pH change
  5. Compensation assessment → appropriate vs inadequate vs mixed
  6. Anion gap (if metabolic acidosis)
  7. Delta-delta (if high AG metabolic acidosis)
  8. Clinical correlation (history, examination, other labs)

Normal Values

ParameterNormal Range
pH7.35–7.45
PaCO₂4.7–6.0 kPa (35–45 mmHg)
HCO₃⁻22–26 mmol/L
Base Excess−2 to +2 mmol/L
Lactate<2 mmol/L
Anion Gap8–12 mmol/L (albumin-corrected: AG + 2.5×(40−Alb g/L))

3. Primary Acid-Base Disorders

DisorderPrimary ChangeCompensationExpected Compensation Formula

| Respiratory Acidosis | ↑ PaCO₂ | ↑ HCO₃⁻ (renal) | Acute: HCO₃⁻ ↑ 1 per 10 PaCO₂ ↑

Chronic: HCO₃⁻ ↑ 4 per 10 PaCO₂ ↑ |

| Respiratory Alkalosis | ↓ PaCO₂ | ↓ HCO₃⁻ (renal) | Acute: HCO₃⁻ ↓ 2 per 10 PaCO₂ ↓

Chronic: HCO₃⁻ ↓ 5 per 10 PaCO₂ ↓ |

Metabolic Acidosis↓ HCO₃⁻↓ PaCO₂ (hyperventilation)Winter's: PaCO₂ = 1.5×HCO₃⁻ + 8 ± 2
Metabolic Alkalosis↑ HCO₃⁻↑ PaCO₂ (hypoventilation)PaCO₂ = 0.7×HCO₃⁻ + 20 ± 5

4. Anion Gap & Delta-Delta

Anion Gap (AG)

AG = Na⁺ − (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻) — albumin-corrected: AG_corr = AG + 2.5 × (40 − Albumin g/L)

AG TypeCauses (MUDPILES)
High AGMethanol, Uraemia, DKA, Paraldehyde, Iron/Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylate
Normal AGDiarrhoea, RTA, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, pancreatic fistula, ureterosigmoidostomy, saline infusion
Low AGHypoalbuminaemia, paraproteinaemia, bromide/lithium toxicity

Delta-Delta (ΔΔ) — Unmasks Mixed Disorders in High AG Acidosis

  • ΔAG = Measured AG − Normal AG (12)
  • ΔHCO₃ = Normal HCO₃ (24) − Measured HCO₃
  • ΔΔ = ΔAG − ΔHCO₃
  • ΔΔ ≈ 0 → pure high AG metabolic acidosis
  • ΔΔ > +6 → concomitant metabolic alkalosis (ΔAG > ΔHCO₃)
  • ΔΔ < −6 → concomitant normal AG metabolic acidosis (ΔHCO₃ > ΔAG)

5. Electrolyte Emergencies

1. Hyperkalaemia (K⁺ >6.5 mmol/L or ECG changes) — CARDIAC EMERGENCY

ECG Changes (Progressive)

K⁺ LevelECG Findings
5.5–6.5Peaked T waves, shortened QT
6.5–7.5PR prolongation, QRS widening, P wave flattening
>7.5Sine wave (QRS merges with T), VT/VF, asystole

Emergency Treatment (Sequential — Minutes Matter)

StepTreatmentDose/DetailsOnsetDuration
1. Membrane StabilisationCalcium gluconate 10%10 mL IV over 2–5 min1–3 min30–60 min
Calcium chloride 10%5–10 mL IV (central)1–3 min30–60 min
2. Shift K⁺ Into CellsInsulin + Dextrose10 U Actrapid + 25g (50 mL 50%) IV15–30 min2–4 h
Salbutamol nebuliser10–20 mg (5 mg × 2–4)15–30 min2 h
Sodium bicarbonate50–100 mmol IV (if acidosis)15–30 min1–2 h
3. Enhance EliminationLoop diuretic (if urine output)Furosemide 40–80 mg IV30 min2–4 h
Sodium polystyrene sulfonate15–30 g PO/PR1–2 h6–12 h
HemodialysisDefinitive (if renal failure/refractory)Immediate—
Priority: Calcium first if ECG changes → Insulin/Dextrose → Dialysis if refractory/renal failure

2. Hyponatraemia (Na⁺ <130 mmol/L) — NEUROLOGICAL EMERGENCY if <120 or symptomatic

Assessment

StepAction
1. Symptoms?Seizures, coma, confusion → emergency
2. Volume statusHypovolaemic / Euvolaemic / Hypervolaemic
3. Urine osmolality>100 = ADH active (SIADH, hypovolaemia, heart failure)
4. Urine Na⁺>20 = renal loss (diuretics, salt-wasting); <20 = extrarenal

Aetiology by Volume

VolumeCauses
HypovolaemicDiuretics, GI losses, cerebral salt wasting, adrenal insufficiency
EuvolaemicSIADH (drugs, malignancy, CNS, pulmonary), hypothyroidism, cortisol deficiency, reset osmostat
HypervolaemicHeart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, CKD

Correction Limits — CRITICAL

ScenarioMax RateMax 24h
Asymptomatic / Chronic0.5 mmol/L/h≤8 mmol/L
Symptomatic (seizures/coma) / Acute (<48h)1–2 mmol/L/h≤10–12 mmol/L
Severe symptomatic3% NaCl 100–150 mL bolus (repeat if needed)≤12 mmol/L
Osmotic Demyelination Risk: overcorrection >10–12 mmol/L in 24h → central pontine myelinolysis (delayed 2–6 days: quadriparesis, dysphagia, locked-in)

Management by Type

TypeTreatment
Hypovolaemic0.9% NaCl (volume + Na⁺)
SIADH (Euvolaemic)Fluid restriction 800–1000 mL/day; 3% NaCl if severe symptomatic; demeclocycline (chronic)
HypervolaemicFluid restriction, loop diuretic, treat underlying (HF, cirrhosis)
Adrenal insufficiencyHydrocortisone 100 mg IV + saline
Cerebral salt wastingVolume + Na⁺ replacement (fludrocortisone)

3. Hypernatraemia (Na⁺ >145 mmol/L) — CELLULAR DEHYDRATION

Assessment

  • Volume status: Hypovolaemic (water loss > Na⁺ loss) / Euvolaemic (pure water loss) / Hypervolaemic (Na⁺ gain > water)
  • Urine osmolality >800 = appropriate ADH; <300 = diabetes insipidus

Correction Limits

ScenarioMax RateMax 24h
Chronic / Asymptomatic0.5 mmol/L/h≤10 mmol/L
Acute (<24h) / Symptomatic1–2 mmol/L/h≤12 mmol/L

Management

TypeTreatment
Hypovolaemic0.9% NaCl → switch to 0.45%/5% dextrose once euvolaemic
Euvolaemic (DI)5% Dextrose or 0.45% NaCl + Desmopressin (central DI)
HypervolaemicLoop diuretic + 5% dextrose; dialysis if severe

4. Hypocalcaemia (Corrected Ca²⁺ <2.0 mmol/L / Ionised <1.0 mmol/L)

ECG: Prolonged QT (ST prolongation), risk Torsades

Acute Symptomatic (tetany, seizure, laryngospasm, QT prolongation):

  • Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV over 10 min (→ 2.25 mmol Ca²⁺)
  • Repeat q10 min until symptoms resolve
  • Monitor: continuous ECG, serum Ca²⁺ q2–4h
  • Correct Mg²⁺ if hypomagnesaemia (refractory without Mg²⁺)

Chronic/Asymptomatic:

  • Oral calcium + vitamin D (alfacalcidol/calcitriol)
  • Treat underlying cause (hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, CKD)

5. Hypercalcaemia (Corrected Ca²⁺ >2.6 mmol/L / Ionised >1.3 mmol/L)

ECG: Shortened QT, bradycardia, AV block

Severity:

  • Mild (2.6–3.0): asymptomatic/mild symptoms
  • Moderate (3.0–3.5): polyuria, nausea, constipation, confusion
  • Severe (>3.5): emergency — coma, AKI, arrhythmias, pancreatitis

Management

  1. IV 0.9% NaCl 2–4 L/day (volume repletion + calciuresis) — target urine output >200 mL/h
  2. Loop diuretic (furosemide 40–80 mg) after volume repletion
  3. Bisphosphonate: Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over 15 min (or Pamidronate 60–90 mg)
  4. Denosumab 120 mg SC (if bisphosphonate contraindicated/renal failure)
  5. Calcitonin 4–8 U/kg SC q6–12h (rapid but tachyphylaxis in 48h)
  6. Dialysis (if severe + renal failure/refractory)

6. Hypomagnesaemia (Mg²⁺ <0.7 mmol/L) — REFRACTORY K⁺/Ca²⁺, ARRHYTHMIAS

ECG: prolonged QT, Torsades risk, VT

Causes: diuretics, PPI, aminoglycosides, alcohol, diarrhoea, malnutrition, refeeding

Treatment:

  • Severe (<0.4 / symptomatic): MgSO₄ 2 g IV over 10–20 min (8 mmol)
  • Moderate: MgSO₄ 10–20 mmol IV over 1–2h, then oral
  • Refractory hypokalaemia → must correct Mg²⁺ first (K⁺ won't correct without Mg²⁺)

7. Hypermagnesaemia (Mg²⁺ >1.5 mmol/L)

Causes: renal failure, Mg²⁺-containing antacids/laxatives, enemas

Signs: loss of reflexes, hypotension, respiratory depression, heart block, cardiac arrest

Treatment: Calcium gluconate 10 mL IV (antagonist), IV fluids + furosemide, dialysis if severe/renal failure

8. Phosphate Disorders

Hypophosphataemia (<0.3 mmol/L severe):

  • Causes: refeeding, alcohol, DKA recovery, respiratory alkalosis, diuretics
  • Effects: muscle weakness, rhabdo, respiratory failure, haemolysis, seizures
  • Treatment: 0.5 mmol/kg IV over 6–12h (monitor Ca²⁺ — hypocalcaemia risk)

Hyperphosphataemia (>2.0 mmol/L):

  • Causes: renal failure, rhabdo, tumour lysis, hypoparathyroidism
  • Treatment: phosphate binders (calcium acetate, sevelamer), dialysis

6. Mixed Acid-Base Disorders — Common in ICU

ClueSuggests
pH normal but abnormal PaCO₂/HCO₃⁻Mixed disorder
Compensation over/underConcomitant opposite disorder
High AG + normal AGDelta-delta >6 (metabolic alkalosis) or <−6 (normal AG acidosis)

...continued (truncated to 200 lines for renderer)

Stepwise Algorithm

5. Electrolyte Emergencies
1. Hyperkalaemia (K⁺ >6.5 mmol/L or ECG changes) — CARDIAC EMERGENCY
2. Hyponatraemia (Na⁺ <130 mmol/L) — NEUROLOGICAL EMERGENCY if <120 or sympto...
3. Hypernatraemia (Na⁺ >145 mmol/L) — CELLULAR DEHYDRATION
