20. Flowchart — MTP Activation

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Major Haemorrhage
↓
MTP Activated by Senior
↓
2x Large-bore IVs, Group O-neg until cross
↓
MTP Pack 1: 4 RBC + 4 FFP + 1 Platelets
↓
Repeat Pack
↓
Calcium 10mL/4 units
↓
TXA 1g IV within 3h
↓
Viscoelastic testing: ROTEM/TEG
↓
Fibrinogen <1.5: Cryoprecipitate 2 pools
↓
Anticoagulant reversal if needed
↓
Surgical Control?
↓
Continue MTP, surgical/radiology
Stop MTP, monitor

21. One-Page Revision Summary

  • Major haemorrhage: ≥4 units/h, SBP <90, HR >110, ongoing bleeding
  • MTP = 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets) = damage control resuscitation
  • TXA 1 g IV within 3 h (CRASH-2)
  • Calcium 10 mL 10% per 4 units RBC
  • Fibrinogen target >1.5 g/L (>2.0 obstetric)
  • Permissive hypotension SBP 80-90 (TBI exception: MAP ≥80)
  • Warm patient (lethal triad: hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy)
  • Warfarin: Vitamin K + PCC; Dabigatran: Idarucizumab; Apixaban/Riva: Andexanet (or PCC)
  • Viscoelastic testing (ROTEM/TEG) for targeted transfusion
  • PPH 4 T's: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • State MTP activation criteria
  • Describe damage control resuscitation
  • List reversal of warfarin, dabigatran, apixaban
  • State TXA dose and timing
  • Outline PPH 4 T's and management

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

22. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Major haemorrhage definition
  • MTP activation criteria
  • 1:1:1 ratio (RBC:FFP:Platelets)
  • TXA 1 g within 3 h
  • Calcium 10 mL per 4 units RBC
  • Fibrinogen target >1.5
  • Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90)
  • Lethal triad (HAC)
  • PPH 4 T's

Should Know

  • Reversal of warfarin, dabigatran, apixaban
  • ROTEM/TEG interpretation
  • FIBTEM MCF target
  • LMWH reversal (protamine partial)
  • TRALI vs TACO
  • PPH pharmacological ladder
  • Permissive hypotension exclusions (TBI)
  • PPH surgical options (B-Lynch, hysterectomy)
  • Stored blood electrolyte issues
  • Citrate toxicity

Nice to Know

  • PROPPR trial details
  • CRASH-2 mortality numbers
  • Andexanet dosing details
  • Viscoelastic test parameters
  • Cryoprecipitate composition
  • EVAR in ruptured AAA
  • rFVIIa (off-label)
  • Fibrinogen concentrate (Riastap)
  • Viscoelastic algorithm details

23. Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

24. Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  • Definition major haemorrhage + MTP
  • Aetiology (trauma, surgical, GI, obstetric, vascular, haematological)
  • Initial assessment (ABCDE)
  • Investigations (FBC, coag, fibrinogen, ABG, ionised Ca, ROTEM/TEG)
  • MTP bundle (1:1:1, TXA, calcium, warm, permissive hypotension)
  • Reversal of anticoagulants
  • PPH 4 T's and management
  • Complications (TRALI, TACO, hyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia, ARDS)
  • Viscoelastic testing

Short Note Skeleton

  • MTP activation criteria
  • Damage control resuscitation
  • TXA dose and timing
  • Reversal of warfarin, DOACs
  • PPH management

Viva One-Liners

  • "MTP: ≥4 units/h or 10 units in 24 h"
  • "DCR: 1:1:1, TXA, calcium, warm, permissive hypotension"
  • "TXA 1 g IV within 3 h (CRASH-2)"
  • "Calcium 10 mL per 4 units RBC"
  • "Fibrinogen target >1.5 g/L"
  • "Permissive hypotension SBP 80-90 (TBI excluded)"
  • "Lethal triad: hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy"
  • "Warfarin reversal: Vit K + PCC"
  • "Dabigatran reversal: Idarucizumab"
  • "Apixaban/rivaroxaban reversal: Andexanet (or PCC)"
  • "PPH 4 T's: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin"

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • Trauma MTP: 1:1:1 + TXA + calcium + warm + permissive hypotension
  • Warfarin + GI bleed: Vit K + PCC + endoscopy
  • DOAC + intracranial bleed: PCC or specific antidote (Andexanet/Idarucizumab)
  • PPH: 4 T's, uterotonics, TXA, balloon, surgery if needed
  • Ruptured AAA: permissive hypotension, MTP, EVAR vs open

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

  • MTP criteria: 4 units/h
  • 1:1:1 ratio
  • TXA 1 g IV <3 h
  • Calcium 10 mL/4 units
  • Fibrinogen >1.5
  • Permissive SBP 80-90 (TBI excluded)
  • HAC = lethal triad
  • Warfarin → Vit K + PCC
  • Dabigatran → Idarucizumab
  • Apixaban/Riva → Andexanet
  • PPH 4 T's

25. Summary

Major haemorrhage = life-threatening bleed requiring rapid, balanced transfusion. MTP activation criteria: ≥4 units RBC in <1 h, ≥10 units in 24 h, or anticipated need (SBP <90, HR >110, base deficit >-6). Damage Control Resuscitation (DCR): 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets) (PROPPR), permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90), avoid crystalloid, TXA 1 g IV within 3 h (CRASH-2), then 1 g over 8 h. Calcium 10 mL 10% per 4 units RBC (citrate toxicity). Fibrinogen target >1.5 g/L (obstetric >2.0) — give cryoprecipitate 2 pools. Lethal triad: Hypothermia + Acidosis + Coagulopathy. Permissive hypotension EXCEPTIONS: TBI (MAP ≥80), pregnancy (SBP 100-110), spinal cord injury. Viscoelastic testing (ROTEM/TEG) for targeted transfusion (FIBTEM MCF >7 mm). Anticoagulant reversal: Warfarin → Vit K 5-10 mg + PCC 25-50 U/kg; Dabigatran → Idarucizumab 5 g; Apixaban/Rivaroxaban → Andexanet (or PCC); Heparin → Protamine 1 mg/100 U; LMWH → Protamine partial. Complications: hypocalcaemia, hyperkalaemia, TRALI, TACO, DIC, ARDS. PPH 4 T's: Tone (70%, atony), Trauma, Tissue (retained), Thrombin. PPH Rx: Oxytocin + Ergometrine + Carboprost + Misoprostol + TXA + balloon/B-Lynch/hysterectomy. Group O-neg until crossmatched.

26. MCQs (10)

  1. Massive transfusion definition:

A. ≥4 units RBC in 24 h

B. ≥10 units RBC in 24 h OR ≥4 units in 1 h

C. ≥20 units in 24 h

D. ≥2 units/h

  1. Damage control resuscitation ratio:

A. 1:1:2 (RBC:FFP:Platelets)

B. 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets)

C. 2:1:1

D. 3:1:0

  1. TXA dose in major trauma:

A. 500 mg IV

B. 1 g IV within 3 h of injury, then 1 g over 8 h

C. 2 g bolus

D. 4 g infusion

  1. Fibrinogen target in major haemorrhage (non-obstetric):

A. >1.0 g/L

B. >1.5 g/L

C. >2.5 g/L

D. >3.0 g/L

  1. Calcium replacement in massive transfusion:

A. 5 mL per 4 units

B. 10 mL of 10% Ca gluconate per 4 units RBC

C. 20 mL per unit

D. Not needed

  1. Permissive hypotension target (non-TBI):

A. SBP 50-60

B. SBP 80-90 mmHg

C. SBP 100-110

D. SBP 120

  1. Warfarin reversal in major bleed:

A. Vitamin K alone

B. Vitamin K 5-10 mg IV + PCC 4-factor 25-50 U/kg

C. FFP alone

D. Protamine

  1. Dabigatran reversal:

A. Andexanet

B. PCC

C. Idarucizumab 5 g IV

D. Vitamin K

  1. PPH 4 T's include all EXCEPT:

A. Tone

B. Trauma

C. Toxins

D. Thrombin

  1. CRASH-2 trial showed TXA in trauma:

A. Increased mortality

B. Reduced all-cause mortality (when given <3 h)

C. No effect

D. Increased VTE

27. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Trauma MTP activated. First pack contents:

A. 4 RBC + 2 FFP + 1 Platelets

B. 4 RBC + 4 FFP + 1 Platelets (1:1:1)

C. 6 RBC + 2 FFP

D. 10 RBC

  1. PPH, uterine atony. First-line uterotonic:

A. Ergometrine

B. Oxytocin 5 U IV

C. Carboprost

D. Misoprostol

  1. Massive transfusion, ionised Ca drops to 0.8 mmol/L. Action:

A. Continue

B. Give 10% Ca gluconate 10 mL IV

C. Stop transfusion

D. Vitamin D

  1. ROTEM FIBTEM MCF <7 mm. Indicates need for:

A. Platelets

B. Fibrinogen (cryoprecipitate or concentrate)

C. FFP

D. RBC

  1. Trauma patient on apixaban with intracranial bleed. Best reversal:

A. Vitamin K

B. Idarucizumab

C. Andexanet alfa (or PCC 4-factor 50 U/kg)

D. Protamine

  1. Permissive hypotension contraindication:

A. Penetrating trauma

B. Traumatic brain injury (need MAP ≥80)

C. Young patient

D. Female patient

  1. PPH, oxytocin and ergometrine failed. Next uterotonic:

A. Carboprost 250 mcg IM (avoid in asthma)

B. Misoprostol

C. Surgery

D. TXA

  1. Major haemorrhage, body temperature 33°C. Action:

A. Continue

B. Active warming (Bair Hugger, fluid warmers, ambient)

C. Cool patient

D. No action needed

  1. Stored blood transfusion causing hyperkalaemia (K 6.8). Rx:

A. Calcium only

B. Insulin/dextrose + calcium + consider dialysis

C. Stop

D. Salbutamol only

  1. Fibrinogen <1.5, obstetric haemorrhage. Target:

A. >1.5

B. >2.0 g/L (higher in obstetric)

C. >2.5

D. >3.0

28. Flashcards

  • Q: Massive transfusion definition

A: ≥10 units RBC in 24 h or ≥4 units in 1 h

  • Q: DCR ratio

A: 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:Platelets)

  • Q: TXA dose

A: 1 g IV within 3 h, then 1 g over 8 h

  • Q: Calcium replacement

A: 10 mL 10% Ca gluconate per 4 units RBC

  • Q: Fibrinogen target

A: >1.5 g/L (obstetric >2.0)

  • Q: Permissive hypotension

A: SBP 80-90 (TBI excluded)

  • Q: Lethal triad

A: Hypothermia, Acidosis, Coagulopathy

  • Q: Warfarin reversal

A: Vitamin K + PCC

  • Q: Dabigatran reversal

A: Idarucizumab 5 g

  • Q: Apixaban reversal

A: Andexanet (or PCC)

  • Q: PPH 4 T's

A: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin

  • Q: FIBTEM MCF <7 mm

A: Fibrinogen needed

29. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — ≥10 units in 24 h or ≥4 in 1 h.

MCQ 2: B — 1:1:1 (PROPPR).

MCQ 3: B — TXA 1 g IV <3 h, then 1 g over 8 h.

MCQ 4: B — >1.5 g/L.

MCQ 5: B — 10 mL Ca gluconate per 4 units RBC.

MCQ 6: B — SBP 80-90.

MCQ 7: B — Vit K + PCC.

MCQ 8: C — Idarucizumab for dabigatran.

MCQ 9: C — "Toxins" not a T; PPH 4 T's are Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin.

MCQ 10: B — CRASH-2 reduced mortality <3 h.

SBA 1: B — 1:1:1 first pack.

SBA 2: B — Oxytocin first.

SBA 3: B — Ca gluconate.

SBA 4: B — FIBTEM MCF <7 mm = low fibrinogen.

SBA 5: C — Andexanet or PCC for apixaban.

SBA 6: B — TBI excluded.

SBA 7: A — Carboprost (avoid in asthma).

SBA 8: B — Active warming.

SBA 9: B — Insulin/dextrose + Ca + dialysis if needed.

SBA 10: B — Fibrinogen target >2.0 in obstetric.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-15

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 10.1.30 Malignant Hyperthermia Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome

Related: [[Distributive Shock]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Status Epilepticus]]

[!important]
Both are rare but fatal hypermetabolic crises. Immediate recognition and specific antidotes save lives.

1. Malignant Hyperthermia (MH)

Definition

Pharmacogenetic disorder of skeletal muscle calcium regulation triggered by volatile anaesthetics or suxamethonium. Uncontrolled Ca²⁺ release → hypermetabolism, rigidity, hyperthermia.

Triggers

  • All volatile agents (sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane, halothane)
  • Suxamethonium (depolarising muscle relaxant)

Clinical Features (Perioperative)

  • Masseter spasm / generalised rigidity (especially after suxamethonium)
  • Rising ETCO₂ (earliest sign)
  • Tachycardia, arrhythmias
  • Hyperthermia (can rise >1°C/5 min, >41°C)
  • Metabolic + respiratory acidosis
  • Rhabdomyolysis → myoglobinuria, hyperkalaemia
  • DIC, renal failure

Immediate Management

  1. STOP TRIGGERS — end volatile, stop suxamethonium
  2. Hyperventilate 100% O₂, high flows
  3. Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV — repeat up to 10 mg/kg (specific antidote, ryanodine receptor antagonist)
  4. Cool actively — ice packs, cold IV fluids, lavage
  5. Treat hyperkalaemia (insulin/dextrose, calcium, bicarbonate)
  6. Monitor: core temp, ABG, CK, electrolytes, ECG, urine output

Red Flags

  • Unexplained rising ETCO₂ + rigidity in theatre
  • Family history of MH / anaesthetic deaths

MH Testing

  • Caffeine-halothane contracture test (muscle biopsy)
  • Genetic testing (RYR1, CACNA1S)

2. Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)

Definition

Idiosyncratic reaction to dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics, antiemetics). Dopamine blockade → hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability.

Culprit Drugs

  • Typical antipsychotics: haloperidol, chlorpromazine, fluphenazine
  • Atypical antipsychotics: risperidone, olanzapine, clozapine
  • Antiemetics: metoclopramide, prochlorperazine
  • Onset: days to weeks after start / dose increase

Clinical Features (FEVER mnemonic)

  • Fever (≥38°C, often >40°C)
  • Encephalopathy (confusion, agitation, coma)
  • Vital sign instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis)
  • Extreme rigidity (lead-pipe, "cogwheel")
  • Raised CK (often massive: >10,000)

Differential

  • MH (anaesthetic trigger)
  • Serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia, serotonergic drugs)
  • Malignant catatonia
  • Sepsis

Diagnosis

  • Clinical (Hunter criteria for serotonin syndrome if confusion + clonus + serotonergic drug)
  • Exclude infection, metabolic, structural
  • CK massively elevated

Management

  1. STOP OFFENDING DRUG
  2. Supportive: cooling, hydration, electrolytes
  3. Dantrolene (also used in NMS, 1–2.5 mg/kg IV q6h)
  4. Bromocriptine 2.5 mg TDS (dopamine agonist)
  5. Amantadine (alternative dopamine agonist)
  6. Avoid dopamine antagonists

3. Comparison Table

FeatureMHNMS
TriggerVolatile anaesthetics, suxamethoniumDopamine antagonists
OnsetMinutes–hours (intraop)Days–weeks
RigidityGeneralised, severeLead-pipe, cogwheel
CKVery highVery high
Specific AntidoteDantroleneDantrolene + Bromocriptine
GeneticRYR1, CACNA1SNo

4. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • MH: rising ETCO₂ + rigidity + hyperthermia in theatre → dantrolene immediately
  • NMS: antipsychotic onset + fever + rigidity + high CK → stop drug + dantrolene + bromocriptine
  • Differentiate NMS from serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia, diarrhoea)
  • Dantrolene works for both

5. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the specific antidote for malignant hyperthermia?
  2. How do you differentiate NMS from serotonin syndrome?
  3. What drugs trigger NMS?

6. One-Page Revision Summary

MH: theatre emergency → stop volatile/sux → dantrolene + cool

NMS: antipsychotic → fever + rigidity + high CK → stop drug → dantrolene + bromocriptine

Both: CK massive, rhabdo risk

7. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • MH: volatile/sux trigger → rising ETCO₂ + rigidity + hyperthermia → dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV
  • NMS: antipsychotic trigger → FEVER (Fever, Encephalopathy, Vital instability, Extreme rigidity, Raised CK) → stop drug + dantrolene + bromocriptine
  • Dantrolene works for both (ryanodine receptor antagonist)
  • Differentiate from serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia, diarrhoea, serotonergic drugs)

Should Know

  • MH: dantrolene up to 10 mg/kg, active cooling, treat hyperkalaemia
  • NMS: bromocriptine 2.5 mg TDS, amantadine alternative
  • MH genetic: RYR1, CACNA1S; caffeine-halothane contracture test
  • NMS onset: days–weeks after start/dose increase

Nice to Know

  • Malignant catatonia differential
  • Prophylactic dantrolene in known MH susceptibility
  • Role of IVIG in severe NMS
  • RyR1 mutations also linked to exertional heat illness

8. MCQs (10)

  1. Specific antidote for malignant hyperthermia:

A. Dantrolene

B. Bromocriptine

C. Dantrolene + bromocriptine

D. Cyproheptadine

  1. MH is triggered by:

A. Antipsychotics

B. Volatile anaesthetics + suxamethonium

C. Serotonergic drugs

D. Antiemetics

  1. Earliest sign of MH in theatre:

A. Hyperthermia

B. Rising ETCO₂

C. Rigidity

D. Arrhythmia

  1. Dantrolene dose in MH:

A. 1 mg/kg IV

B. 2.5 mg/kg IV (repeat up to 10 mg/kg)

C. 5 mg/kg IV

D. 10 mg/kg IV single dose

  1. NMS is triggered by:

A. Volatile anaesthetics

B. Dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics, antiemetics)

C. Serotonergic drugs

D. Antibiotics

  1. FEVER mnemonic for NMS — E stands for:

A. Encephalopathy

B. Electrolytes

C. ECG changes

D. Emesis

  1. Key differentiator: NMS vs Serotonin syndrome:

A. NMS has clonus, hyperreflexia, diarrhoea

B. Serotonin syndrome has lead-pipe rigidity

C. Serotonin syndrome has clonus, hyperreflexia, diarrhoea

D. No difference

  1. Bromocriptine dose in NMS:

A. 1 mg TDS

B. 2.5 mg TDS

C. 5 mg TDS

D. 10 mg TDS

  1. MH genetic testing:

A. RYR1, CACNA1S

B. SCN1A, SCN2A

C. DMD, DYSTROPHIN

D. No genetic basis

  1. Dantrolene mechanism:

A. Dopamine agonist

B. Ryanodine receptor antagonist (inhibits Ca²⁺ release)

C. Serotonin antagonist

D. Calcium channel blocker

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. During general anaesthesia, a patient develops masseter spasm, rising ETCO₂ (from 35 to 65 mmHg), tachycardia, and temperature rising to 39.5°C. Most likely diagnosis:

A. NMS

B. Malignant hyperthermia

C. Sepsis

D. Thyroid storm

  1. Immediate management of the above patient:

A. Continue anaesthesia, give paracetamol

B. Stop volatile/sux, hyperventilate 100% O₂, dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV

C. Give bromocriptine

D. Cool only

  1. A 30-year-old on haloperidol for 2 weeks presents with fever 40°C, lead-pipe rigidity, confusion, CK 25,000. Diagnosis:

A. Malignant hyperthermia

B. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome

C. Serotonin syndrome

D. Sepsis

  1. Management of the above patient:

A. Continue haloperidol, give dantrolene

B. Stop haloperidol, dantrolene + bromocriptine 2.5 mg TDS, supportive

C. Cyproheptadine

D. L-dopa

  1. A patient on sertraline started on tramadol develops fever, clonus, hyperreflexia, diarrhoea, agitation. Diagnosis:

A. NMS

B. Malignant hyperthermia

C. Serotonin syndrome

D. Sepsis

  1. Serotonin syndrome treatment (severe):

A. Dantrolene

B. Cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) + supportive

C. Bromocriptine

D. L-dopa

  1. A known MH-susceptible patient requires emergency surgery. Prophylaxis:

A. Avoid all anaesthetics

B. Total IV anaesthesia (TIVA) — propofol, opioid, avoid volatile/sux; dantrolene available

C. Normal anaesthesia with dantrolene standby

D. Cancel surgery

  1. CK in MH and NMS:

A. Normal

B. Mildly elevated

C. Very high (often >10,000)

D. Only elevated in NMS

  1. Malignant catatonia vs NMS:

A. Catatonia has fever, rigidity, autonomic instability, responds to lorazepam

B. Catatonia = NMS

C. Catatonia responds to bromocriptine

D. No difference

  1. Dantrolene works for both MH and NMS because:

A. It is a dopamine agonist

B. It is a ryanodine receptor antagonist (stabilises Ca²⁺ release)

C. It is a serotonin antagonist

D. It cools the patient

10. Flashcards

  • Q: MH trigger

A: Volatile anaesthetics + suxamethonium

  • Q: MH earliest sign

A: Rising ETCO₂

  • Q: MH antidote

A: Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV (up to 10 mg/kg)

  • Q: MH genetics

A: RYR1, CACNA1S

  • Q: NMS trigger

A: Dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics, antiemetics)

  • Q: NMS mnemonic

A: FEVER (Fever, Encephalopathy, Vital instability, Extreme rigidity, Raised CK)

  • Q: NMS management

A: Stop drug + dantrolene + bromocriptine 2.5 mg TDS

  • Q: Serotonin syndrome

A: Clonus, hyperreflexia, diarrhoea, serotonergic drugs → cyproheptadine

  • Q: Dantrolene mechanism

A: Ryanodine receptor antagonist → inhibits Ca²⁺ release

  • Q: MH prophylaxis

A: TIVA (avoid volatile/sux), dantrolene available

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: A — Dantrolene specific antidote for MH.

MCQ 2: B — Volatile anaesthetics + suxamethonium.

MCQ 3: B — Rising ETCO₂ earliest sign.

MCQ 4: B — 2.5 mg/kg IV, repeat up to 10 mg/kg.

MCQ 5: B — Dopamine antagonists.

MCQ 6: A — Encephalopathy.

MCQ 7: C — Serotonin syndrome = clonus, hyperreflexia, diarrhoea.

MCQ 8: B — 2.5 mg TDS.

MCQ 9: A — RYR1, CACNA1S.

MCQ 10: B — Ryanodine receptor antagonist.

SBA 1: B — Masseter spasm + rising ETCO₂ + hyperthermia in theatre = MH.

SBA 2: B — Stop triggers, hyperventilate, dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV.

SBA 3: B — Antipsychotic + fever + rigidity + high CK = NMS.

SBA 4: B — Stop offending drug + dantrolene + bromocriptine.

SBA 5: C — Sertraline + tramadol = serotonergic → serotonin syndrome.

SBA 6: B — Cyproheptadine (5-HT₂A antagonist) for severe serotonin syndrome.

SBA 7: B — TIVA (propofol/opioid), avoid volatile/sux, dantrolene available.

SBA 8: C — CK very high in both (>10,000).

SBA 9: A — Catatonia responds to lorazepam; NMS responds to dantrolene/bromocriptine.

SBA 10: B — Ryanodine receptor antagonist works for both (Ca²⁺ dysregulation common pathway).

▸ 10.1.31 Neutropenic Sepsis

Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]]

[!important]
Neutropenic sepsis is a medical emergency with high mortality. Treat immediately — do not wait for cultures.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define neutropenic sepsis
  • Apply the "door-to-needle" time target
  • Choose appropriate first-line antibiotics
  • Know when to escalate antifungals

2. Definition

  • Temperature ≥38°C (or <35.5°C) AND
  • Neutrophil count <0.5 × 10⁹/L (or predicted to fall below)

3. Immediate Management (Door-to-Needle <1 hour)

  1. Blood cultures (peripheral + line if present)
  2. IV antibiotics immediately — do not delay
  3. Metabolic screen: FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, lactate, blood glucose
  4. CXR if respiratory symptoms

4. First-Line Antibiotics (per local policy)

  • Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS (most common)
  • Meropenem 1g IV TDS (if penicillin allergy / ESBL risk)
  • Add glycopeptide (vancomycin/teicoplanin) if: line infection, MRSA colonised, hypotensive, skin/soft tissue infection

5. Risk Stratification (MASCC / CISNE scores)

  • Low risk (MASCC ≥21): oral antibiotics possible, outpatient consideration
  • High risk (MASCC <21): inpatient IV antibiotics, monitor closely

6. Duration

  • Afebrile 48–72 hours + neutrophils >0.5 × 10⁹/L
  • Total 5–7 days typically
  • If no source identified + afebrile 5 days, stop antibiotics

7. Antifungal Escalation

  • Add if persistent fever >4–7 days on broad-spectrum antibiotics
  • Empirical caspofungin or amphotericin B

8. Red Flags

  • Hypotension, altered consciousness, respiratory failure
  • Focus of infection requiring surgical drainage
  • Severe mucositis / typhlitis

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Never delay antibiotics for cultures
  • Piperacillin-tazobactam is standard first-line
  • MASCC score guides admission vs outpatient
  • Line cultures alone are insufficient — need peripheral too

10. Common Viva Questions

  1. Define neutropenic sepsis.
  2. What is the door-to-needle target?
  3. When would you add vancomycin?
  4. When do you escalate to antifungals?

11. One-Page Revision Summary

Neutropenic sepsis = fever + neutrophils <0.5

Antibiotics within 1 hour

Piperacillin-tazobactam standard

MASCC score for risk stratification

12. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Definition: fever ≥38°C + neutrophils <0.5
  • Door-to-needle <1 hour
  • Piperacillin-tazobactam first-line
  • MASCC score for risk stratification

Should Know

  • When to add glycopeptide (vancomycin)
  • Antifungal escalation criteria (>4–7 days)
  • Duration: afebrile 48–72h + neutrophils >0.5

Nice to Know

  • CISNE score alternative
  • Outpatient management criteria (low risk MASCC)
  • Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) role

13. MCQs (10)

  1. Neutropenic sepsis definition requires:

A. Temperature ≥38°C only

B. Neutrophils <0.5 × 10⁹/L only

C. Temperature ≥38°C AND neutrophils <0.5

D. Temperature ≥39°C AND neutrophils <1.0

  1. Door-to-needle antibiotic target in neutropenic sepsis:

A. <30 minutes

B. <1 hour

C. <2 hours

D. <4 hours

  1. First-line antibiotic for neutropenic sepsis (most common):

A. Meropenem

B. Piperacillin-tazobactam

C. Ceftriaxone

D. Vancomycin

  1. When do you ADD vancomycin to first-line therapy?

A. Always

B. Only if penicillin allergy

C. Line infection, MRSA, hypotension, skin/soft tissue infection

D. After 48 hours

  1. MASCC score ≥21 indicates:

A. High risk — inpatient IV antibiotics

B. Low risk — oral/outpatient possible

C. Need for antifungal

D. Need for ICU

  1. Antifungal escalation if persistent fever after:

A. 24 hours

B. 48 hours

C. 4–7 days

D. 14 days

  1. Duration of antibiotics if no source found and afebrile:

A. 24 hours

B. 48 hours

C. 5 days

D. 14 days

  1. Blood cultures in neutropenic sepsis:

A. Not needed if antibiotics started

B. Peripheral only

C. Peripheral + line (if present)

D. Line only

  1. Empirical antifungal options:

A. Fluconazole

B. Caspofungin or amphotericin B

C. Voriconazole

D. Itraconazole

  1. Neutropenic typhlitis is:

A. Caecal inflammation in neutropenia

B. Oral mucositis

C. Perianal abscess

D. Fungal pneumonia

14. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 45-year-old man on day 10 of chemotherapy for AML presents with temperature 38.5°C. Neutrophils 0.2. BP 110/70. Best immediate action:

A. Wait for blood cultures, then start antibiotics

B. Start piperacillin-tazobactam IV immediately, take cultures simultaneously

C. Start oral ciprofloxacin

D. Observe for 2 hours

  1. A 60-year-old woman with neutropenic sepsis has a Hickman line. She is hypotensive (BP 85/50). Antibiotics should include:

A. Piperacillin-tazobactam only

B. Piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin

C. Meropenem only

C. Ceftriaxone + metronidazole

  1. A patient with neutropenic sepsis has MASCC score 22. She is clinically stable. Appropriate management:

A. Admit for IV antibiotics

B. Oral antibiotics, outpatient follow-up possible

C. Add antifungal immediately

D. ICU admission

  1. Persistent fever on day 6 of broad-spectrum antibiotics in neutropenic patient. Neutrophils still 0.1. Next step:

A. Change antibiotics

B. Add empirical antifungal (caspofungin)

C. Stop antibiotics

D. Repeat blood cultures only

  1. A patient with neutropenic sepsis becomes hypotensive after antibiotics started. Lactate 4.5. Management:

A. Continue antibiotics only

B. Fluid resuscitation + vasopressors + ICU referral

C. Add antifungal

D. Change antibiotics

  1. Which is NOT an indication for adding vancomycin?

A. MRSA colonisation

B. Skin/soft tissue infection

C. Hypotension

D. Fever >39°C alone

  1. Duration of antibiotics for documented Gram-negative bacteraemia in neutropenia:

A. 3 days

B. 7–10 days

C. 14–21 days

D. 28 days

  1. A patient with neutropenic sepsis has severe mucositis. Risk of:

A. Bacteraemia

B. Fungal translocation

C. Both A and B

D. Neither

  1. G-CSF in neutropenic sepsis:

A. Routine for all

B. Not routinely recommended (consider in high-risk/prolonged)

C. Contraindicated

D. Only if fungal infection

  1. Empirical meropenem is used instead of piperacillin-tazobactam when:

A. Always

B. Penicillin allergy or ESBL risk

C. Only in ICU

D. Never

15. Flashcards

  • Q: Neutropenic sepsis definition

A: Temp ≥38°C (or <35.5°C) + neutrophils <0.5 × 10⁹/L

  • Q: Door-to-needle target

A: <1 hour

  • Q: First-line antibiotic

A: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS

  • Q: When to add vancomycin

A: Line infection, MRSA, hypotension, skin/soft tissue infection

  • Q: MASCC ≥21

A: Low risk — oral/outpatient possible

  • Q: Antifungal escalation

A: Persistent fever >4–7 days on broad-spectrum antibiotics

16. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: C — Both fever AND neutropenia required.

MCQ 2: B — Door-to-needle <1 hour.

MCQ 3: B — Piperacillin-tazobactam standard.

MCQ 4: C — Line, MRSA, hypotension, skin/soft tissue.

MCQ 5: B — MASCC ≥21 = low risk.

MCQ 6: C — 4–7 days persistent fever.

MCQ 7: C — 5 days if no source + afebrile.

MCQ 8: C — Peripheral + line (if present).

MCQ 9: B — Caspofungin or amphotericin B.

MCQ 10: A — Caecal inflammation = typhlitis.

SBA 1: B — Immediate antibiotics + cultures simultaneously.

SBA 2: B — Line + hypotension → add vancomycin.

SBA 3: B — MASCC ≥21 = low risk, outpatient possible.

SBA 4: B — Add empirical antifungal at day 4–7.

SBA 5: B — Septic shock = fluids + vasopressors + ICU.

SBA 6: D — Fever alone not indication for vancomycin.

SBA 7: B — 7–10 days typical for documented bacteraemia.

SBA 8: C — Mucositis = portal for both bacteria and fungi.

SBA 9: B — Not routine; consider high-risk/prolonged neutropenia.

SBA 10: B — Penicillin allergy or ESBL risk.

▸ 10.1.32 Non Invasive Ventilation Niv

Related: [[Acute Respiratory Failure]], [[ABCDE Approach and Initial Assessment]]

[!important]
NIV reduces the need for intubation and improves outcomes in selected patients with acute respiratory failure.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Know the indications and contraindications for NIV
  • Choose between CPAP and BiPAP
  • Monitor for NIV failure
  • Know when to escalate to intubation

2. Indications

Strong evidence

  • COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35)
  • Cardiogenic pulmonary oedema
  • Immunocompromised patients with hypoxaemic respiratory failure

Other uses

  • Obesity hypoventilation syndrome
  • Weaning from invasive ventilation

3. CPAP vs BiPAP

ModeIndicationTypical Settings
CPAPPulmonary oedema, atelectasis5–15 cmH₂O
BiPAPCOPD, hypercapnic failureIPAP 10–20, EPAP 4–8

4. Contraindications

  • Cardiac/respiratory arrest
  • GCS <8 or inability to protect airway
  • Vomiting / high aspiration risk
  • Facial trauma or burns
  • Untreated pneumothorax

5. Monitoring & Failure Criteria

  • Reassess at 1–2 hours
  • Worsening pH, rising PaCO₂, persistent hypoxia
  • Increasing respiratory rate or distress
  • Haemodynamic instability

6. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Start NIV early in COPD with acidosis — do not wait for severe acidosis
  • CPAP is first choice in acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema
  • Always have a plan for intubation if NIV fails

7. Common Viva Questions

  1. What are the indications for NIV in acute respiratory failure?
  2. When would you choose CPAP over BiPAP?
  3. What are the signs of NIV failure?

8. One-Page Revision Summary

NIV = CPAP or BiPAP

Indicated in COPD acidosis + cardiogenic oedema

Contraindicated if low GCS or aspiration risk

Monitor closely — intubate if failing

9. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Indications: COPD exacerbation (pH<7.35), cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, immunocompromised
  • CPAP for oedema/atelectasis (5–15 cmH₂O); BiPAP for COPD/hypercapnia (IPAP 10–20, EPAP 4–8)
  • Contraindications: arrest, GCS<8, aspiration risk, facial trauma, untreated pneumothorax
  • Monitor at 1–2h: worsening pH, rising PaCO₂, exhaustion → intubate

Should Know

  • Early NIV in COPD — don't wait for severe acidosis
  • HFNO (high-flow nasal oxygen) as alternative in some hypoxaemic failure
  • Weaning: SIMV → PS → CPAP trial

Nice to Know

  • NIV in do-not-intubate patients (palliative)
  • Home NIV for OHS/COPD
  • Helmet NIV vs mask

10. MCQs (10)

  1. Strongest evidence for NIV in acute respiratory failure:

A. Asthma

B. COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis

C. Pneumonia alone

D. PE

  1. BiPAP settings for COPD:

A. CPAP 10

B. IPAP 10–20, EPAP 4–8

C. CPAP 20

D. IPAP 5, EPAP 15

  1. CPAP first-line in:

A. COPD

B. Cardiogenic pulmonary oedema

C. Asthma

D. Neuromuscular disease

  1. Contraindication to NIV:

A. COPD pH 7.30

B. GCS <8 / unable to protect airway

C. Cardiogenic oedema

D. Immunocompromised

  1. NIV failure criteria at 1–2 hours:

A. pH improving

B. Worsening pH, rising PaCO₂, persistent hypoxia, exhaustion

C. RR <20

D. SpO₂ 95%

  1. Immunocompromised patient with hypoxaemic failure — NIV:

A. Contraindicated

B. Indicated (avoids intubation complications)

C. Only if CD4 >200

D. Only in ICU

  1. BiPAP vs CPAP — BiPAP provides:

A. CPAP only

B. IPAP + EPAP (pressure support)

C. Higher FiO₂ only

D. No difference

  1. Facial trauma — NIV:

A. Safe

B. Contraindicated (mask seal/leaks)

C. Preferred

D. Helmet only

  1. Untreated pneumothorax + NIV:

A. Helps re-expand

B. Contraindicated (worsens tension)

C. Safe with low pressures

D. Only if chest drain planned

  1. Weaning from invasive ventilation using NIV:

A. Not possible

B. Possible (SIMV → PS → CPAP trial)

C. Contraindicated

D. Only in COPD

11. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 68-year-old COPD exacerbation, pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 8.5, RR 35. Best initial NIV:

A. CPAP 10

B. BiPAP IPAP 12, EPAP 4

C. High-flow O₂ only

D. Intubate immediately

  1. A 75-year-old acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, hypoxic, crackles. NIV:

A. BiPAP IPAP 15, EPAP 5

B. CPAP 10 cmH₂O

C. No NIV — intubate

D. HFNO only

  1. A 50-year-old immunocompromised post-chemo, bilateral infiltrates, hypoxic. NIV:

A. Contraindicated

B. Trial of NIV (avoids intubation)

C. Intubate immediately

D. CPAP only

  1. A COPD patient on BiPAP for 2h. pH 7.18 (was 7.28), PaCO₂ rising, exhausted. Next:

A. Increase IPAP

B. Intubate (NIV failure)

C. Switch to CPAP

D. Continue 4 more hours

  1. A patient with GCS 8, respiratory failure. NIV:

A. Start BiPAP

B. Contraindicated — intubate (GCS <8)

C. CPAP trial

D. HFNO

  1. NIV mask leak — management:

A. Ignore

B. Re-fit, try different size, consider helmet

C. Increase pressures

D. Intubate

  1. High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) vs NIV:

A. HFNO = NIV

B. HFNO for hypoxaemic failure; NIV for hypercapnic (COPD) + cardiogenic oedema

C. NIV only

D. HFNO contraindicated

  1. A patient vomiting on NIV. Action:

A. Continue, suction

B. Stop NIV — aspiration risk → intubate

C. Give antiemetic, continue

D. Turn to side

  1. Obesity hypoventilation syndrome — home NIV:

A. Not indicated

B. Indicated (BiPAP overnight)

C. CPAP only

D. Oxygen only

  1. Reassessment time after NIV start:

A. 4–6 hours

B. 1–2 hours

C. 12 hours

D. 24 hours

12. Flashcards

  • Q: NIV indications (strong evidence)

A: COPD acidosis (pH<7.35), cardiogenic oedema, immunocompromised

  • Q: BiPAP settings

A: IPAP 10–20, EPAP 4–8

  • Q: CPAP settings

A: 5–15 cmH₂O

  • Q: CPAP first-line for

A: Cardiogenic pulmonary oedema

  • Q: Contraindications

A: Arrest, GCS<8, aspiration risk, facial trauma, untreated PTX

  • Q: NIV failure at 1–2h

A: Worsening pH, rising PaCO₂, persistent hypoxia, exhaustion

  • Q: Immunocompromised

A: NIV indicated (avoids intubation)

  • Q: HFNO vs NIV

A: HFNO for Type 1; NIV (BiPAP) for Type 2 / COPD

13. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — COPD acidosis = strongest NIV evidence.

MCQ 2: B — BiPAP = IPAP + EPAP.

MCQ 3: B — CPAP for cardiogenic oedema (reduces preload/afterload).

MCQ 4: B — GCS<8 = airway risk = contraindicated.

MCQ 5: B — Failure = worsening pH/CO₂, hypoxia, exhaustion.

MCQ 6: B — NIV avoids intubation in immunocompromised.

MCQ 7: B — BiPAP = pressure support (IPAP-EPAP).

MCQ 8: B — Facial trauma = mask seal issues.

MCQ 9: B — Untreated PTX = NIV worsens tension.

MCQ 10: B — NIV used for weaning (PS/CPAP trials).

SBA 1: B — COPD acidosis = BiPAP first-line.

SBA 2: B — Cardiogenic oedema = CPAP.

SBA 3: B — Immunocompromised = NIV trial to avoid intubation.

SBA 4: B — pH falling, rising CO₂, exhausted = intubate.

SBA 5: B — GCS<8 = airway risk = intubate.

SBA 6: B — Mask leak = re-fit, helmet.

SBA 7: B — HFNO for hypoxaemic; NIV for hypercapnic/COPD.

SBA 8: B — Vomiting = aspiration risk = intubate.

SBA 9: B — OHS = home BiPAP overnight.

SBA 10: B — Reassess at 1–2h.

13. Answer Key with Explanations
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 10.1.33 Obstructive Shock

Related: [[Shock - Overview]], [[Cardiogenic Shock]], [[Pulmonary Embolism]]

[!important]
Obstructive shock = mechanical obstruction to blood flow (either into or out of the heart). Rapid diagnosis and relief of obstruction is lifesaving.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise the three main causes of obstructive shock
  • Use bedside ultrasound for rapid diagnosis
  • Know the specific interventions for each cause

2. Main Causes

  1. Massive Pulmonary Embolism
  2. Cardiac Tamponade
  3. Tension Pneumothorax

3. Clinical Differentiation

FeatureMassive PETamponadeTension PTX
JVPRaisedRaisedRaised
Heart soundsLoud P2MuffledNormal
TracheaCentralCentralDeviated
Breath soundsNormalNormalReduced
Pulsus paradoxusPossiblePresentPossible
Bedside USRV strainPericardial effusionLung sliding absent

4. Management

Massive PE

  • Thrombolysis (alteplase) if unstable
  • Consider ECMO or surgical embolectomy

Cardiac Tamponade

  • Urgent pericardiocentesis
  • Fluid bolus while preparing

Tension Pneumothorax

  • Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS mid-clavicular line)
  • Followed by chest drain

5. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis — do not wait for CXR
  • Tamponade shows pulsus paradoxus and electrical alternans
  • Massive PE often has clear lungs despite severe shock

6. Common Viva Questions

  1. How do you differentiate the causes of obstructive shock?
  2. What is the immediate management of tension pneumothorax?
  3. When would you give thrombolysis in PE?

7. One-Page Revision Summary

Obstructive = mechanical blockage

PE → thrombolysis

Tamponade → pericardiocentesis

Tension PTX → needle decompression (clinical diagnosis)

8. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Three causes: massive PE, cardiac tamponade, tension pneumothorax
  • All cause raised JVP (obstructed venous return)
  • Tension PTX = clinical diagnosis (don't wait for CXR)
  • Immediate interventions: PE=thrombolysis, Tamponade=pericardiocentesis, Tension PTX=needle decompression

Should Know

  • Beck's triad (tamponade): hypotension, raised JVP, muffled heart sounds
  • Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg in tamponade (also severe asthma, PE)
  • RV strain on echo = massive PE
  • Electrical alternans on ECG = tamponade (large effusion)

Nice to Know

  • Surgical pericardial window vs pericardiocentesis
  • VA-ECMO for massive PE
  • REBOA not for obstructive shock

9. MCQs (10)

  1. Obstructive shock is caused by:

A. Pump failure

B. Mechanical obstruction to blood flow

C. Vasodilatation

D. Hypovolaemia

  1. Three main causes of obstructive shock:

A. PE, Tamponade, Tension PTX

B. MI, PE, Tamponade

C. PE, Sepsis, Tension PTX

D. MI, Sepsis, Anaphylaxis

  1. Tension pneumothorax — JVP:

A. Low

B. Raised (impaired venous return)

C. Normal

D. Not assessable

  1. Tension pneumothorax — trachea:

A. Central

B. Deviated AWAY from affected side

C. Deviated TOWARDS affected side

D. Not displaced

  1. Tension pneumothorax — immediate management:

A. Chest X-ray first

B. Needle decompression 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line (clinical diagnosis)

C. Chest drain only

C. Intubate first

  1. Cardiac tamponade — Beck's triad:

A. Hypertension, raised JVP, loud heart sounds

B. Hypotension, raised JVP, muffled heart sounds

C. Hypotension, low JVP, muffled sounds

D. Hypertension, low JVP, loud sounds

  1. Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg seen in:

A. Only tamponade

B. Tamponade, severe asthma, massive PE, obstructive airway

C. Only PE

C. Only asthma

  1. Massive PE on bedside echo:

A. LV dilation

B. RV dilation/strains (RV/LV >1), McConnell's sign

C. Pericardial effusion

D. Normal

  1. Electrical alternans on ECG suggests:

A. Massive PE

B. Cardiac tamponade (large effusion swinging heart)

C. Tension PTX

D. MI

  1. Tension pneumothorax — breath sounds:

A. Normal

B. Reduced/absent on affected side

C. Bronchial breathing

D. Wheeze only

10. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 25-year-old trauma, sudden hypotension, trachea deviated right, absent breath sounds left. Immediate action:

A. Chest X-ray

B. Needle decompression left 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line

C. Chest drain left side

D. Intubate

  1. A 60-year-old with metastatic cancer, dyspnoea, hypotension, raised JVP, muffled heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus 20 mmHg. Echo: large pericardial effusion. Best management:

A. IV fluids only

B. Urgent pericardiocentesis

C. Pericardial window surgery

D. Diuretics

  1. A 40-year-old sudden dyspnoea, hypotension, clear lungs, raised JVP, loud P2, RV dilation on echo. Most likely:

A. Tamponade

B. Massive PE

C. Tension PTX

D. Cardiogenic shock

  1. Massive PE with hypotension (SBP 80/50) — thrombolysis:

A. Contraindicated

B. Indicated (alteplase 100mg over 2h) if no contraindication

C. Only after CT

D. Never

  1. Tension PTX needle decompression — site:

A. 4th ICS mid-axillary

B. 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line

C. 5th ICS mid-axillary

D. Anywhere

  1. A patient with hypotension, raised JVP, Kussmaul's sign, clear lungs. Echo: RV dilation, IVC dilated non-collapsing. Diagnosis:

A. Tamponade

B. Constrictive pericarditis (or RV failure — but think obstructive)

C. PE

D. Tension PTX

  1. Pericardiocentesis — approach:

A. Left parasternal

B. Subxiphoid (echo-guided preferred)

C. Right parasternal

D. Apical

  1. Massive PE — contraindication to thrombolysis:

A. Age >70

B. Active bleeding, recent stroke, surgery <2 weeks

C. Hypertension

D. Renal impairment

  1. Pulsus paradoxus measurement:

A. SBP difference >10 mmHg between inspiration/expiration

B. DBP difference

C. Pulse rate difference

D. MAP difference

  1. A patient with known pericardial effusion develops sudden hypotension, muffled sounds, electrical alternans. Next:

A. Observe

B. Emergency pericardiocentesis

C. Start noradrenaline

D. CT chest

11. Flashcards

  • Q: Obstructive shock causes

A: Massive PE, Cardiac tamponade, Tension pneumothorax

  • Q: Tension PTX — clinical dx

A: Don't wait for CXR — needle decompress 2nd ICS mid-clavicular

  • Q: Tamponade — Beck's triad

A: Hypotension + raised JVP + muffled heart sounds

  • Q: Tamponade — pulsus paradoxus

A: >10 mmHg drop SBP on inspiration

  • Q: Massive PE — echo

A: RV dilation (RV/LV >1), McConnell's sign

  • Q: Electrical alternans

A: Tamponade (large effusion)

  • Q: PE thrombolysis

A: Alteplase 100mg over 2h if unstable

  • Q: All obstructive shock = raised JVP

A: Yes (venous return obstructed)

12. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Mechanical obstruction to flow.

MCQ 2: A — PE, Tamponade, Tension PTX.

MCQ 3: B — JVP raised (venous return blocked).

MCQ 4: B — Trachea deviated AWAY from tension PTX.

MCQ 5: B — Clinical diagnosis, immediate needle decompression.

MCQ 6: B — Beck's: hypotension, raised JVP, muffled sounds.

MCQ 7: B — Tamponade, severe asthma, PE, obstructive airway.

MCQ 8: B — RV strain (RV/LV >1), McConnell's sign (RV free wall akinesis with apex sparing).

MCQ 9: B — Electrical alternans = tamponade (Swinging heart in large effusion).

MCQ 10: B — Reduced/absent breath sounds on affected side.

SBA 1: B — Trauma + tracheal deviation + absent breath sounds = tension PTX → immediate needle decompression.

SBA 2: B — Tamponade with pulsus paradoxus + hypotension = urgent pericardiocentesis.

SBA 3: B — Hypotension + clear lungs + raised JVP + RV dilation = massive PE.

SBA 4: B — Unstable massive PE = thrombolysis if no contraindication.

SBA 5: B — 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line for needle decompression.

SBA 6: B — Kussmaul's + RV dilation + clear lungs = constrictive or RV failure (obstructive physiology).

SBA 7: B — Subxiphoid echo-guided preferred for pericardiocentesis.

SBA 8: B — Active bleeding, recent stroke, recent surgery = absolute contraindications.

SBA 9: A — SBP drop >10 mmHg on inspiration.

SBA 9: B — Electrical alternans + tamponade signs = emergency pericardiocentesis.

5. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points
6. Common Viva Questions
7. One-Page Revision Summary

Must Know
Should Know
Nice to Know

▸ 10.1.34 Raised Intracranial Pressure

Related: [[Coma and Altered Consciousness]], [[Status Epilepticus]], [[Critical Care Monitoring]], [[Acute Medicine in Pregnancy]]

[!important]
Raised ICP is a life-threatening emergency causing cerebral ischaemia and brainstem herniation. Monro-Kellie doctrine: skull is fixed-volume (brain 80%, blood 10%, CSF 10%); any ↑ in one component must be compensated by ↓ in others or ICP rises. Normal ICP 5–15 mmHg (≤20 in adults). Cushing's triad = hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations. Management: head-up 30°, osmotherapy (mannitol/hypertonic saline), controlled ventilation (PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa), treat underlying cause. Key FCPS/MRCP: Monro-Kellie, Cushing's triad, herniation syndromes, osmotherapy, hyperventilation as rescue only.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define raised ICP and its pathophysiology (Monro-Kellie)
  • Identify causes (focal, diffuse)
  • Recognise clinical signs (Cushing's triad, herniation syndromes)
  • Apply stepwise management (positioning, osmotherapy, ventilation)
  • Know indications for surgical decompression
  • Differentiate from mimics

2. Definition

  • Normal ICP: 5–15 mmHg (supine)
  • Sustained >20 mmHg = raised ICP (pathological)
  • Refractory >25 mmHg despite treatment
  • Brain Trauma Foundation: treat ICP >22 mmHg (target <22)
  • Cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) = MAP − ICP; target CPP 60–70 mmHg

3. Monro-Kellie Doctrine

The skull is a fixed-volume container with 3 components:

Component% VolumeCompensation
Brain parenchyma80%Limited (compliance)
Blood10%Vasoconstriction, venous drainage
CSF10%Displacement into spinal canal

Any increase in one component must be offset by decrease in another (or ICP rises). When compliance exhausted, small volume changes cause large ICP rises.

4. Aetiology

Intracranial (3 categories)

CategoryExamples
Focal massHaematoma (extradural, subdural, intracerebral), tumour, abscess
Diffuse brain swellingTraumatic brain injury (TBI), hypoxic-ischaemic injury, encephalitis, fulminant hepatic failure, Reye syndrome, DKA
CSF flow obstructionHydrocephalus (obstructive, communicating), Chiari, aqueduct stenosis
VenousCerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST)

Extracranial Causes

  • Hypoxia, hypercarbia (cerebral vasodilation)
  • Hyponatraemia (cerebral oedema)
  • Hepatic/renal failure (encephalopathy, oedema)
  • Malignant hypertension (PRES)
  • High altitude cerebral oedema

5. Pathophysiology

  1. Volume increase (mass, blood, oedema, CSF)
  2. Initial compensation: CSF displacement, venous collapse
  3. Compliance exhausted → ICP rises steeply
  4. Reduced CPP → cerebral ischaemia
  5. Herniation syndromes (see below)
  6. Cushing's reflex: systemic hypertension → reflex bradycardia (preserves CPP)
  7. Brainstem compression → irregular respirations, death

6. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Headache (worse on lying, coughing, Valsalva; worse in morning)
  • Vomiting (often without nausea — "positional")
  • Visual disturbance (transient obscurations, diplopia — CN VI palsy from stretch)
  • Altered consciousness (lethargy → coma)
  • Seizures

Signs

  • Papilloedema (often late — absent in acute rises)
  • Cushing's triad (late, ominous):
  1. Hypertension (widened pulse pressure)
  2. Bradycardia
  3. Irregular respirations (Cheyne-Stokes, central neurogenic hyperventilation, apneustic, ataxic)
  • Pupillary changes (uncal herniation: ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil)
  • Decorticate / decerebrate posturing
  • Upward gaze palsy (Parinaud — dorsal midbrain)

Cushing's Triad — Explained

  • Hypertension = compensatory to maintain CPP
  • Bradycardia = baroreceptor response to hypertension
  • Irregular respirations = brainstem (medulla) compression
  • All three = impending herniation — treat immediately

7. Herniation Syndromes

SyndromeCauseFeatures
Uncal (transtentorial)Temporal lobe through tentorial notchIpsilateral CN III palsy (fixed dilated pupil), contralateral hemiparesis, decreased consciousness
Central transtentorialDiffuse downward pressureBilateral pinpoint pupils → midposition fixed, decorticate → decerebrate, Cushing's, death
Tonsillar (coning)Cerebellar tonsils through foramen magnumNeck stiffness, cardiac arrest, sudden death
Subfalcine (cingulate)Cingulate gyrus under falxContralateral leg weakness, ACA territory infarct
Upward transtentorialPosterior fossa mass pushing upComa, small pupils, Parinaud's

8. Investigations

  • CT head (urgent, non-contrast): mass effect, midline shift, effaced sulci, basal cisterns
  • MRI brain: better for posterior fossa, infiltrative disease
  • CT venogram / MRV: if CVST suspected
  • LP — contraindicated if raised ICP suspected (herniation risk); only after imaging
  • Fundoscopy: papilloedema (chronic), but may be absent in acute
  • ICP monitoring: gold standard (ventricular catheter, parenchymal bolt) — for TBI in ICU
  • ABG, U&E, glucose, LFT, ammonia, ABG: identify extracranial causes
  • EEG: non-convulsive status epilepticus

9. Management

Step 1: ABCDE + Position

  • Airway: intubate if GCS ≤8 or deteriorating
  • Breathing: controlled ventilation, target PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa (35–38 mmHg)
  • Head-up 30°, head midline (avoid jugular compression)
  • Loosen neck ties / cervical collars (venous drainage)
  • Avoid hypotension: MAP target for CPP

Step 2: Osmotherapy

AgentDoseOnsetNotes
Mannitol 20%0.5–1 g/kg IV bolus (250–500 mL)15–30 minOsmotic diuretic; monitor UO, Na⁺
Hypertonic saline 3%150–250 mL IV over 10–20 min5–15 minPreferred in hypovolaemia; target Na⁺ 145–155
Hypertonic saline 23.4%30 mL IV over 10–20 minRapidCentral line required
Hold mannitol if serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg (renal failure risk)

Step 3: Sedation + Analgesia

  • Propofol (reduces cerebral metabolism, ICP)
  • Fentanyl / morphine (analgesia, ICP ↓)
  • Midazolam if paralysed

Step 4: Treat Underlying Cause

  • Mass lesion → neurosurgical decompression
  • Hydrocephalus → external ventricular drain (EVD) / shunt
  • CVST → anticoagulation (LMWH even with haemorrhage)
  • Infection → antibiotics, abscess drainage
  • Metabolic → correct sodium, glucose, ammonia
  • TBI → decompressive craniectomy if refractory

Step 5: Rescue Therapies (Refractory ICP)

  • Hyperventilation to PaCO₂ 4.0–4.5 kPa (transient only — vasoconstriction, risk of ischaemia; do NOT <4.0 kPa)
  • Therapeutic hypothermia (35–36°C) — controversial, rebound ICP
  • Barbiturate coma (thiopentone) — last resort, EEG monitoring
  • Decompressive craniectomy (e.g., DECRA trial — refractory ICP)
  • External ventricular drain (EVD) for hydrocephalus
  • Paralysis (cisatracurium infusion)

Step 6: Supportive Care

  • DVT prophylaxis (mechanical, then LMWH when safe)
  • Seizure prophylaxis (levetiracetam — phenytoin not in TBI)
  • Stress ulcer prophylaxis (PPI)
  • Glycaemic control (6–10 mmol/L)
  • Temperature control (fever worsens ICP)
  • Maintain CPP 60–70 (vasopressors if needed)
  • Avoid: hypoxia, hyperthermia, hyponatraemia, hyperglycaemia, hypovolaemia, anaemia

10. Red Flags

  • Cushing's triad (impending herniation)
  • Fixed dilated pupil
  • GCS drop >2 points
  • Decerebrate posturing
  • Apnoea / irregular respirations

11. Complications

  • Brainstem herniation → death
  • Cerebral ischaemia / infarction
  • Hydrocephalus (post-haemorrhagic, post-meningitis)
  • Hypopituitarism (pituitary apoplexy, surgery)
  • Central venous thrombosis (dehydration, immobility)
  • Visual loss (prolonged papilloedema, optic atrophy)

12. Prognosis

  • Outcome depends on cause, duration, CPP, age
  • TBI: GCS, pupillary response, age, CT findings (Marshall classification) predict outcome
  • 6-month outcome: GOSE (Extended Glasgow Outcome Scale)

13. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  1. Monro-Kellie doctrine: skull fixed volume — brain 80%, blood 10%, CSF 10%
  2. Cushing's triad: HT + Brady + Irregular RR = impending herniation
  3. Normal ICP: 5–15 mmHg; treat >20–22 mmHg
  4. CPP = MAP − ICP; target 60–70 mmHg
  5. Head-up 30° + head midline
  6. Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg OR 3% saline 150–250 mL for osmotherapy
  7. Hyperventilation to PaCO₂ 35–38 mmHg (do NOT <30)
  8. Drain CSF via EVD if hydrocephalus
  9. Decompressive craniectomy for refractory ICP (DECRA)
  10. Avoid hypoxia, hyperthermia, hyponatraemia, anaemia
  11. Pupillary changes (CN III) = uncal herniation
  12. LP contraindicated without imaging

14. Common Viva Questions

  1. Define raised ICP. What is the Monro-Kellie doctrine?
  2. List causes of raised ICP
  3. Describe Cushing's triad and pathophysiology
  4. Outline the management of raised ICP step-by-step
  5. What are the herniation syndromes?
  6. How do you calculate CPP? What is the target?
  7. Indications for decompressive craniectomy
  8. Why avoid LP in suspected raised ICP?

15. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Confusing ICP and CPP targets — CPP = MAP − ICP, target 60–70
  • Hyperventilation below PaCO₂ 4.0 kPa → cerebral ischaemia
  • Mannitol in hypovolaemia → hypoperfusion; use hypertonic saline
  • Hypotonic fluids (5% dextrose) worsen cerebral oedema — use 0.9% NaCl
  • LP without imaging → tonsillar herniation
  • Head-down positioning → ↑ ICP
  • Sedation reduces ICP but must be titrated, not withheld
  • Steroids in TBI no benefit; only for vasogenic oedema (tumour)
  • Hypoxia + hypercapnia → cerebral vasodilation → ↑ ICP

16. Mnemonics

  • MONRO-KELLIE: 3 compartments — Brain 80%, Blood 10%, CSF 10%
  • CUSHING's triad: Hypertension, Bradycardia, Irregular RR
  • CPP = MAP − ICP, target 60–70
  • MANNITOL: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg; Na⁺ 145–155 if hypertonic saline
  • UNCAL HERNIATION: CN III palsy (fixed dilated pupil) + contralateral hemiparesis
  • CINGULATE/SUBFALCINE: contralateral leg weakness
  • Tonsillar: sudden death
  • TBI steroids: NO (CRASH trial)
  • LP contraindications: raised ICP, mass lesion, coagulopathy, infection at site
  • Avoid: hypotonic fluids, hypoxia, hyperthermia, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia

17. Mind Map

Raised ICP
Definition
Normal 5-15 mmHg
Treat >20-22 mmHg
CPP = MAP - ICP (target 60-70)
Monro-Kellie
Skull fixed volume
Brain 80%, Blood 10%, CSF 10%
Compensation by displacement
Causes
Mass: Haematoma, Tumour, Abscess
Diffuse: TBI, Encephalitis, FHF, DKA
CSF: Hydrocephalus
Venous: CVST
Extracranial: Hyponatraemia, Hypoxia, Hypercapnia
Clinical Features
Symptoms: Headache, Vomiting, Visual
Signs: Papilloedema (chronic)
Cushing's Triad: HT + Brady + Irregular RR
Pupils: Uncal (CN III)
Posturing: Decorticate, Decerebrate
Herniation
Uncal: CN III + contralateral hemiparesis
Central: Downward pressure
Tonsillar: Sudden death
Subfalcine: Contralateral leg
Management
ABCDE + Position (30deg)
Sedation + Analgesia
Osmotherapy
– Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg
– Hypertonic saline 3% 150-250ml
PaCO2 35-38 mmHg
Treat underlying cause
EVD for hydrocephalus
Rescue: Hyperventilate, Barbiturates, Decompressive craniectomy
Supportive: DVT, Seizure Ppx, Temp control
Avoid
LP without imaging
Hypotonic fluids
Steroids in TBI
PaCO2 <30 mmHg

18. Flowchart — Raised ICP Management

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Raised ICP Suspected
↓
ABCDE + Head-up 30 deg + Head midline
↓
Intubate if GCS <=8 + PaCO2 35-38 mmHg
↓
Sedation + Analgesia Propofol + Fentanyl
↓
Osmotherapy: Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg OR 3% Saline 150-250ml
↓
CT Head: Identify Cause
↓
Neurosurgical Referral + Decompression
EVD Insertion
Continue medical management
↓
ICP Still >25?
↓
Rescue: Brief Hyperventilation + Paralysis
Barbiturate coma OR Decompressive craniectomy

19. One-Page Revision Summary

  • Normal ICP: 5–15 mmHg; treat >20–22 mmHg
  • CPP = MAP − ICP; target 60–70
  • Monro-Kellie: Brain 80%, Blood 10%, CSF 10% in fixed skull
  • Cushing's triad: HT + Bradycardia + Irregular RR = herniation imminent
  • Uncal: CN III palsy (fixed dilated pupil) + contralateral hemiparesis
  • Tonsillar: sudden death
  • Stepwise management: ABCDE + Head-up 30° → Sedation → Osmotherapy (mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg OR 3% saline 150–250 mL) → PaCO₂ 35–38 mmHg → EVD for hydrocephalus → Decompressive craniectomy
  • Avoid: LP without imaging, hypotonic fluids, steroids in TBI, PaCO₂ <30 mmHg
  • Treat underlying cause (mass, hydrocephalus, CVST, infection)

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • State Monro-Kellie doctrine
  • List Cushing's triad and its significance
  • Outline the management of raised ICP
  • Describe uncal herniation signs
  • Calculate CPP and state target

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

20. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Monro-Kellie doctrine
  • Cushing's triad
  • CPP calculation and target (60–70)
  • Stepwise management: head-up, sedation, osmotherapy, ventilation
  • Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg + hypertonic saline 3% doses
  • Herniation syndromes (uncal, tonsillar, central, subfalcine)
  • Avoid LP without imaging

Should Know

  • Decompressive craniectomy (DECRA trial)
  • Barbiturate coma for refractory ICP
  • EVD for hydrocephalus
  • Avoid steroids in TBI
  • PaCO₂ target 35–38 mmHg

Nice to Know

  • ICP monitoring devices (ventricular catheter, parenchymal bolt)
  • Therapeutic hypothermia (controversial)
  • GOSE outcome scale
  • Marshall CT classification in TBI
  • PRES, hepatic encephalopathy specifics

21. Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

22. Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  • Definition of raised ICP
  • Monro-Kellie doctrine
  • Aetiology (mass, diffuse, CSF, venous, extracranial)
  • Pathophysiology
  • Clinical features (symptoms + signs)
  • Cushing's triad explanation
  • Herniation syndromes
  • Investigations (CT, MRI, ICP monitoring)
  • Stepwise management
  • Complications + prognosis

Short Note Skeleton

  • Monro-Kellie doctrine diagram
  • Cushing's triad box
  • Herniation syndromes table
  • Osmotherapy doses
  • ICP/CPP targets

Viva One-Liners

  • "Normal ICP 5–15 mmHg, treat >20"
  • "CPP = MAP − ICP, target 60–70"
  • "Cushing's triad = HT + Brady + Irregular RR"
  • "Uncal: CN III palsy + contralateral hemiparesis"
  • "Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg OR 3% saline 150–250 mL"
  • "PaCO₂ target 35–38 mmHg"
  • "Head-up 30°, head midline"
  • "LP contraindicated without imaging"
  • "Monro-Kellie: brain 80, blood 10, CSF 10"
  • "Tonsillar herniation = sudden death"

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • TBI, GCS 6, blown right pupil → intubate, mannitol, urgent CT, neurosurgical decompression
  • Subarachnoid haemorrhage, GCS 13, severe headache → CT angio, nimodipine, EVD if hydrocephalus
  • Fulminant hepatic failure, confusion, hyperventilation → ammonia, treat as raised ICP, hypertonic saline
  • Post-op craniopharyngioma, Na⁺ 128, GCS 10 → hyponatraemia, DDAVP withheld, fluid restrict, MRI

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

  • ICP 5–15; treat >20
  • CPP = MAP − ICP, target 60–70
  • Monro-Kellie: 80/10/10
  • Cushing's: HT + Brady + Irregular RR
  • Uncal: CN III + contralateral hemi
  • Head-up 30° + head midline
  • Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg OR 3% saline 150–250 mL
  • PaCO₂ 35–38 mmHg
  • No LP without imaging
  • No steroids in TBI

23. Summary

Raised ICP is a life-threatening emergency causing cerebral ischaemia and brainstem herniation. Monro-Kellie doctrine: skull is a fixed-volume container (brain 80%, blood 10%, CSF 10%); any ↑ in one component must be offset by ↓ in another. Normal ICP: 5–15 mmHg; treat >20–22 mmHg. CPP = MAP − ICP; target 60–70 mmHg. Cushing's triad (late, ominous): hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations. Herniation syndromes: uncal (CN III palsy + contralateral hemiparesis), central, tonsillar (sudden death), subfalcine. Stepwise management: ABCDE + head-up 30° + head midline → intubation + controlled ventilation (PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa / 35–38 mmHg) → sedation + analgesia → osmotherapy (mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg OR hypertonic saline 3% 150–250 mL) → identify and treat underlying cause (mass → surgical decompression; hydrocephalus → EVD) → rescue therapies (brief hyperventilation, paralysis, barbiturate coma, decompressive craniectomy). Avoid: LP without imaging, hypotonic fluids, steroids in TBI (CRASH), PaCO₂ <30 mmHg. Supportive: DVT/PUD prophylaxis, seizure prophylaxis, temperature control, glycaemic control.

24. MCQs (10)

  1. Monro-Kellie doctrine: cranial cavity components in proportion:

A. Brain 70%, Blood 20%, CSF 10%

B. Brain 80%, Blood 10%, CSF 10%

C. Brain 60%, Blood 30%, CSF 10%

D. Brain 90%, Blood 5%, CSF 5%

  1. Cushing's triad includes all EXCEPT:

A. Hypertension

B. Bradycardia

C. Irregular respirations

D. Tachycardia

  1. Normal ICP in adults:

A. 0–5 mmHg

B. 5–15 mmHg

C. 20–30 mmHg

D. 30–40 mmHg

  1. CPP is calculated by:

A. ICP − MAP

B. MAP − ICP

C. MAP + ICP

D. MAP / ICP

  1. Target CPP in raised ICP:

A. 30–40 mmHg

B. 50–60 mmHg

C. 60–70 mmHg

D. 80–90 mmHg

  1. Mannitol dose in raised ICP:

A. 0.1 g/kg

B. 0.5–1 g/kg

C. 2 g/kg

D. 5 g/kg

  1. Uncal herniation classically presents with:

A. Pinpoint pupils bilaterally

B. Ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil + contralateral hemiparesis

C. Cheyne-Stokes respiration

D. Bilateral leg weakness

  1. PaCO₂ target in controlled ventilation for raised ICP:

A. 25–30 mmHg

B. 35–38 mmHg

C. 45–50 mmHg

D. 50–55 mmHg

  1. Steroids in raised ICP due to traumatic brain injury:

A. Recommended at high dose

B. Recommended at low dose

C. Not recommended (CRASH trial)

D. Recommended only in children

  1. LP is contraindicated in suspected raised ICP because of risk of:

A. Infection

B. Tonsillar herniation

C. Bleeding

D. Hypotension

25. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 25-year-old with severe TBI, intubated, ICP 28 mmHg, MAP 80 mmHg. CPP is:

A. 50 mmHg

B. 52 mmHg (below target 60–70)

C. 100 mmHg

D. 108 mmHg

  1. A patient with raised ICP has Cushing's triad. Most likely herniation type:

A. Subfalcine

B. Tonsillar / uncal

C. Upward

D. None

  1. Hypertonic saline 3% dose in raised ICP:

A. 50 mL

B. 150–250 mL over 10–20 min

C. 1 L

D. 5 mL

  1. A patient with obstructive hydrocephalus and raised ICP — best treatment:

A. Mannitol only

B. External ventricular drain (EVD)

C. Lumbar puncture

D. Steroids

  1. Cushing's triad is a sign of:

A. Early raised ICP

B. Impending herniation (late)

C. Hyperventilation

D. Sleep

  1. Mannitol should be held if serum osmolality exceeds:

A. 280 mOsm/kg

B. 300 mOsm/kg

C. 320 mOsm/kg

D. 350 mOsm/kg

  1. A patient with fulminant hepatic failure develops confusion. Most likely raised ICP cause:

A. Mass lesion

B. Cerebral oedema (hyperammonaemia)

C. Hydrocephalus

D. CVST

  1. Brief hyperventilation in raised ICP:

A. Sustained therapy

B. Rescue therapy only (transient, risk of ischaemia)

C. First-line

D. Never used

  1. Decompressive craniectomy is indicated in:

A. Refractory raised ICP despite medical management

B. All TBI

C. Stroke

D. Meningitis only

  1. A patient on mannitol develops hypovolaemia. Better alternative:

A. More mannitol

B. Hypertonic saline 3%

C. 5% dextrose

D. 0.45% saline

26. Flashcards

  • Q: Monro-Kellie doctrine

A: Brain 80%, Blood 10%, CSF 10% in fixed skull

  • Q: Normal ICP

A: 5–15 mmHg (treat >20)

  • Q: CPP formula

A: CPP = MAP − ICP

  • Q: CPP target

A: 60–70 mmHg

  • Q: Cushing's triad

A: Hypertension + Bradycardia + Irregular respirations

  • Q: Mannitol dose

A: 0.5–1 g/kg IV

  • Q: Hypertonic saline 3% dose

A: 150–250 mL over 10–20 min

  • Q: PaCO₂ target

A: 35–38 mmHg (4.5–5.0 kPa)

  • Q: Uncal herniation

A: CN III palsy (fixed dilated pupil) + contralateral hemiparesis

  • Q: Tonsillar herniation

A: Sudden death

  • Q: Head position in raised ICP

A: Head-up 30°, head midline

  • Q: Steroids in TBI

A: NOT recommended (CRASH trial)

  • Q: Decompressive craniectomy

A: Refractory raised ICP despite medical management

27. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Monro-Kellie: brain 80%, blood 10%, CSF 10%.

MCQ 2: D — Cushing's = HT + Brady + Irregular RR (NOT tachycardia).

MCQ 3: B — Normal ICP 5–15 mmHg.

MCQ 4: B — CPP = MAP − ICP.

MCQ 5: C — CPP target 60–70 mmHg.

MCQ 6: B — Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg.

MCQ 7: B — Uncal: ipsilateral CN III + contralateral hemiparesis.

MCQ 8: B — PaCO₂ 35–38 mmHg.

MCQ 9: C — CRASH trial: steroids ↑ mortality in TBI.

MCQ 10: B — LP can cause tonsillar herniation.

SBA 1: B — CPP = 80 − 28 = 52 mmHg, below target.

SBA 2: B — Cushing's = impending herniation.

SBA 3: B — 150–250 mL over 10–20 min.

SBA 4: B — EVD is definitive for hydrocephalus.

SBA 5: B — Cushing's = late/impending herniation.

SBA 6: C — Hold mannitol if osmolality >320.

SBA 7: B — Hyperammonaemia causes cerebral oedema in FHF.

SBA 8: B — Brief hyperventilation = rescue only.

SBA 9: A — Refractory raised ICP despite medical management.

SBA 10: B — Hypertonic saline 3% — preferred in hypovolaemia.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-15

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 10.1.35 Rhabdomyolysis

Related: [[Acute Kidney Injury in Critical Illness]], [[Acid-Base and Electrolyte Emergencies]], [[Malignant Hyperthermia Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome]]

[!important]
Rhabdomyolysis = skeletal muscle breakdown releasing myoglobin, CK, K⁺, phosphate, urate into circulation. CK >5× ULN (>1000 U/L) is diagnostic; CK often >5000 in established rhabdo. Triad: muscle pain + weakness + dark (tea-coloured) urine. Major complications: AKI (myoglobinuric) in 10-50%, hyperkalaemia (life-threatening arrhythmia), compartment syndrome, DIC, hypocalcaemia (early, then hypercalcaemia recovery). Treatment: AGGRESSIVE IV fluids early (1-2 L/h initially, target UO 200-300 mL/h, total 10-12 L/d). Urine alkalinisation (NaHCO₃, pH >6.5) controversial. Mannitol, loop diuretics in severe cases. Dialysis if AKI/refractory hyperK. FCPS/MRCP: causes (crush, prolonged immobility, drugs, malignant hyperthermia, NMS, statins, exertion, alcohol, snake bite), CK thresholds, AKI mechanism, treatment, complications.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define rhabdomyolysis
  • Identify common causes (trauma, exertional, drugs, metabolic, genetic)
  • Recognise clinical features (muscle pain, weakness, dark urine)
  • Investigate (CK, myoglobin, U&E, urate, LDH, AST/ALT, urinalysis)
  • Manage: aggressive IV fluids, urine alkalinisation, electrolyte correction
  • Prevent and treat complications (AKI, compartment syndrome, hyperK, DIC)
  • Identify when to dialyse
  • Counsel on statin-induced rhabdo

2. Definition

  • Rhabdomyolysis = skeletal muscle injury with release of intracellular contents (myoglobin, CK, K⁺, phosphate, urate, LDH, AST) into systemic circulation
  • Diagnostic CK threshold: >5× ULN (>1000 U/L); some use >5000 U/L for "significant"
  • Severe rhabdo: CK >5000 U/L OR CK >10,000 U/L with AKI risk

3. Pathophysiology

  • Direct muscle injury (crush, trauma) → membrane disruption → myoglobin, CK, electrolytes release
  • ATP depletion → Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase failure → cell swelling, Ca²⁺ influx → proteases, phospholipases → cell death
  • Myoglobin → kidney injury:
  • Renal vasoconstriction
  • Tubular obstruction (cast formation)
  • Oxidative damage (ferryl myoglobin, lipid peroxidation)
  • Worsened by hypovolaemia, acidosis, low UO
  • Release of K⁺ → hyperkalaemia
  • Phosphate release → binds Ca²⁺ → hypocalcaemia
  • Hepatic haptoglobin binds free myoglobin → cleared (saturable)

4. Causes (Mnemonic: "CRUSH MY LEGS" or systematic)

Trauma / Compression

  • Crush injury (RTA, building collapse, industrial)
  • Prolonged immobility (stroke on floor, intoxicated, post-op)
  • Compartment syndrome
  • Burns, electrical injury
  • Severe exertion (marathon, military training, seizures)
  • Physical torture, abuse

Exertional / Environmental

  • Heat stroke (exertional or classic)
  • Strenuous exercise (marathon, weightlifting, status epilepticus)
  • Status asthmaticus (work of breathing)
  • Severe dystonia, tetanus, neuroleptic malignant syndrome
  • Hypothermia

Drugs / Toxins

  • Statins (especially with fibrates, CYP inhibitors, hypothyroidism)
  • Antipsychotics (NMS — clozapine, haloperidol)
  • Antidepressants (SSRI, TCA, MAOI — serotonin syndrome)
  • Alcohol (acute intoxication, chronic)
  • Illicit: cocaine, amphetamines, heroin, MDMA
  • Snake/spider bite (viper, brown recluse)
  • Carbon monoxide, cyanide
  • Salicylate, paracetamol
  • Colchicine
  • Daptomycin

Metabolic / Endocrine

  • DKA, HHS (osmotic, phosphate)
  • Severe electrolyte abnormalities: K⁺, Na⁺, Ca²⁺, phosphate
  • Hypothyroidism (statin risk)
  • Cushing's, hyperaldosteronism
  • Inborn errors: McArdle's (GSD V), carnitine palmitoyltransferase II def, mitochondrial

Inflammatory / Autoimmune

  • Polymyositis, dermatomyositis
  • Vasculitis (polyarteritis nodosa)
  • SLE, RA

Infectious

  • Influenza, COVID-19, HIV, EBV, CMV, HSV
  • Staph, Strep, Clostridium (gas gangrene)
  • Legionella

Genetic / Muscular Dystrophies

  • Duchenne, Becker
  • Limb-girdle, facioscapulohumeral
  • Rhabdomyolysis with metabolic stress

5. Clinical Features

Classic Triad

  • Muscle pain (myalgia) — often severe
  • Muscle weakness
  • Dark, tea-coloured urine (myoglobinuria) — visible when >100 mg/dL myoglobin

Other Features

  • Muscle swelling, tenderness
  • Stiffness, contracture
  • Fever (inflammatory)
  • Nausea, vomiting (electrolyte disturbance)
  • Confusion, agitation (electrolyte, uraemia)
  • Cardiac arrhythmia (hyperK)
  • Oliguria / anuria (AKI)
  • Compartment syndrome signs (pain out of proportion, pain on passive stretch, tense swelling, paresthesia, pulselessness late)

6. Investigations

Lab

TestResult in Rhabdo
CK (creatine kinase)>1000 U/L (5× ULN), often >10,000; peaks 24-72 h
Myoglobin↑ in serum + urine (urine dipstick + for blood, no RBCs)
U&EK⁺ ↑, urea ↑, Cr ↑ (AKI), phosphate ↑
Calcium↓ early (binds phosphate), ↑ late (recovery)
Urate↑
LDH, AST, ALT↑ (muscle source)
Aldolase↑
DIC screenPT/PTT ↑, fibrinogen ↓, D-dimer ↑
UrinalysisBlood + on dipstick (myoglobin), no RBCs on microscopy
ABGMetabolic acidosis (high anion gap)
Creatinine↑ in AKI (CK release cross-reacts)

Imaging

  • CT/MRI of muscle (suspected compartment, deep muscle involvement)
  • Compartment pressure measurement if suspected
  • ECG: peaked T, wide QRS, sine wave (hyperK)

Other (Specific Causes)

  • Toxicology screen (drugs, alcohol)
  • TSH, free T4 (thyroid)
  • VBG/lactate (DKA, sepsis)
  • Autoimmune screen (polymyositis)
  • Genetic testing (recurrent)

7. Diagnosis

  • Clinical suspicion + CK >1000 U/L (5× ULN) + features
  • Urine dipstick + for blood without RBCs (myoglobin) — sensitive but non-specific
  • Exclude MI (CK-MB), stroke, thyroid storm

8. Complications

ComplicationMechanismManagement
AKI (10-50%)Myoglobin nephrotoxic, hypovolaemiaAggressive fluids, dialysis
HyperkalaemiaK⁺ release, AKICa gluconate, insulin/dextrose, dialysis
Hypocalcaemia (early)Phosphate binding Ca²⁺Generally NO replacement (causes ectopic calcification)
Hypercalcaemia (recovery)Mobilised Ca²⁺Hydration, bisphosphonates if severe
Compartment syndromeMuscle swellingFasciotomy
DICTissue factor, thromboplastin releaseFFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets
ArrhythmiaHyperK, electrolyte disturbanceTreat hyperK
Hepatic dysfunctionShock, cytokinesSupportive
ARDSSIRSSupportive ventilation
SepsisTissue necrosis, infectionAntibiotics, source control

9. Management

Initial (within 6 hours ideally)

  1. ABC + IV access (2 large-bore)
  2. Aggressive IV crystalloid (0.9% saline): 1-2 L bolus, then 200-500 mL/h
  • Target UO: 200-300 mL/h (adult)
  • Some centres: maintain UO 3-5 mL/kg/h
  1. Treat hyperkalaemia: Ca gluconate 10% 10 mL IV, insulin/dextrose
  2. Monitor: UO, U&E, CK (6-12 h), Ca²⁺, ECG
  3. Correct hypovolaemia (often 10-12 L/day needed)
  4. Drainage if localised muscle bleed (e.g., femoral)

Ongoing

  • Continue IV fluids until CK <1000 U/L AND UO stable
  • Urine alkalinisation (controversial): NaHCO₃ 100-150 mmol/L in fluid; target urine pH >6.5 (some use >7.0)
  • Theoretical benefit: less myoglobin precipitation
  • Concerns: volume overload, hypocalcaemia, alkalosis
  • Not routinely recommended (Brown 2004)
  • Mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV (controversial; if severe, no diuresis despite fluids)
  • Reduces muscle swelling, tubular obstruction
  • Use if fluid overload risk with crystalloid
  • Loop diuretics (furosemide) ONLY after volume repletion; not first-line
  • Dialysis for refractory AKI/hyperK (not prevented by myoglobin clearance — CK is cleared)

Specific Treatments by Cause

  • Malignant hyperthermia: dantrolene 2-2.5 mg/kg IV bolus, then 1 mg/kg q6h
  • NMS: dantrolene, bromocriptine, supportive, dantrolene or amantadine
  • Serotonin syndrome: cyproheptadine, cooling
  • Heat stroke: cooling, dantrolene controversial
  • Statin-induced: stop statin, supportive
  • Snake bite: antivenom
  • Crush injury: MTP if active bleed, surgical debridement
  • Compartment syndrome: fasciotomy (urgent)

Indications for Dialysis

  • AEIOU: Acidosis, Electrolytes (hyperK), Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia
  • Standard AKI criteria (KDIGO 2-3 with complications)

10. Prevention

  • Early IV fluids at scene (crush injury)
  • Cool patient (heat illness)
  • Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, contrast)
  • DVT prophylaxis when appropriate
  • Statin monitoring (baseline CK, with symptoms)
  • Avoid prolonged immobility (turn stroke patients)
  • Dantrolene for MH-susceptible surgery

11. Prognosis

  • Most recover with supportive care
  • AKI recovery: usually within 1-3 weeks
  • Mortality: 5-25% in severe rhabdo (especially with multi-organ failure)
  • Long-term: chronic kidney disease risk, recurrent rhabdo (genetic causes)

12. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  1. Rhabdo = skeletal muscle breakdown with intracellular release
  2. CK >5× ULN (>1000 U/L) diagnostic; >5000 severe
  3. Triad: muscle pain + weakness + dark urine
  4. Urine dipstick + for blood, NO RBCs on microscopy (myoglobin)
  5. Major complications: AKI (10-50%), hyperK, compartment syndrome, DIC
  6. Aggressive IV fluids EARLY (1-2 L/h, target UO 200-300 mL/h, 10-12 L/d)
  7. Hyperkalaemia is life-threatening
  8. Early hypocalcaemia (don't replace — causes ectopic calcification)
  9. Late hypercalcaemia during recovery
  10. Urine alkalinisation controversial (not routine)
  11. Mannitol in severe cases (controversial)
  12. Loop diuretics only after euvolaemia
  13. Compartment syndrome → fasciotomy
  14. Malignant hyperthermia → dantrolene
  15. Statin + fibrate = rhabdo risk

13. Common Viva Questions

  1. Define rhabdomyolysis
  2. List common causes
  3. Classic triad
  4. Investigations
  5. Complications (AKI, hyperK)
  6. Initial management
  7. Aggressive IV fluids rationale
  8. Indications for dialysis
  9. Compartment syndrome
  10. Statin-induced rhabdo

14. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • CK >1000 = diagnostic threshold (5× ULN)
  • Urine dipstick + for blood, NO RBCs = myoglobin
  • DON'T replace calcium early (causes ectopic calcification)
  • HyperK is the immediate life threat
  • Fluids EARLY (within 6 h = best outcome)
  • Urine alkalinisation controversial; not routine
  • Mannitol controversial
  • Loop diuretics not first-line (only after euvolaemia)
  • Compartment syndrome = clinical diagnosis; measure pressure if unsure
  • Dantrolene for MH, NMS, heat stroke
  • Statin alone rarely causes rhabdo; + fibrate, hypothyroidism ↑ risk
  • CK peaks 24-72 h
  • AKI mortality higher in elderly, sepsis, multi-organ failure
  • Crush injury: aggressive fluids at scene
  • Recurrent rhabdo = genetic workup (McArdle, CPT II)

15. Mnemonics

  • Causes: CRASH (Crush, Rhabdomyolysis of statin/Seizures, Alcohol, Statins, Heat stroke) + Snake bite, Immobility, Trauma, Exertion, NMS, Hypothyroidism
  • Triad: Pain + Weakness + Dark urine
  • CK threshold: 5× ULN (>1000)
  • Urine: Dipstick + for blood, NO RBCs
  • Initial Rx: Aggressive IV fluids (1-2 L/h, UO 200-300 mL/h)
  • DON'T replace early Ca²⁺
  • Complications: AKI + HyperK + Compartment + DIC
  • Dialysis: AEIOU (Acidosis, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia)
  • MH/NMS: Dantrolene
  • Statin + fibrate: high risk

17. Flowchart — Rhabdo Management

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Rhabdo
↓
CK, UandE, urate, Ca, Mg, ECG, urinalysis
↓
CK >1000?
↓
Reassess
Aggressive IV 0.9% Saline 1-2 L/h
↓
Target UO 200-300 mL/h
↓
Monitor CK 6-12h, UandE, ECG
↓

↓

↓

↓
